fr:mainmatter.html:p: I have been using todoist and habitify. I decided on these tools primarily because of their API availability. I wanted to be able to add / complete / see outstanding tasks or habits for the day without the allure of everything else my browser has to offer. Also visualization for my Weeknotes.

html:ul.html:li: I had Claude write a little script to have my next tasks listed when clicked in my status bar, so I do not have to open the browser to check what I need to do today.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: It seems how to properly use these (todoist in particular) is the subject of much debate. Here, I will outline what I converged to / works for ME right now. I have broken down my system into a hierarchy:

html:ol.html:li: Higher level: how to actually choose what to do.
Lower level: how to actually do the chosen task.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is effectively a task management system.

Work streams. I took this name from my investment banker friends (sounds cool / important). Basically, these are groupings I have made and allocated time for each day. They roughly consist of the following:

html:ul.html:li: Research / work (4hrs first thing in the morning):
Active work towards new artifact or paper i.e. coding up ideas.
In the absence of ideas, studying existing relevant papers.
Deliberate practice (2hrs after run / lunch):
Name taken from Cal Newport.
As of writing this, a current example is spending ~2hrs a day writing / optimizing known kernels in CuTe / Pallas / NKI.
Basically, something I need to understand or master for research that is not novel.
Extraneous Study (30min - 1hr):
Things not directly related to research, but I am curious about.
I try to maintain study of related computer science fields. Primarily, studying a textbook in something like OS, computer architecture, etc.
Habits / daily upkeep (1hr normally end of day):
Mainly consists of reviewing Anki cards daily that come from extraneous study / deliberate practice.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To decide on the next task, I use a priority-based system:

html:ol.html:li: Things I will be working on today / until they are done. At most ONE thing per work stream.
The backlog of things to do next

html:ul.html:li: An index to find things to move to priority 1 for the next day
Keep relatively small to avoid the crippling nature of choice - roughly on the order of things I want done this week.

html:ol.html:li: A larger backlog of things - should be completed in the next month or so.
Things I am not working on right now, or have any need to be completed any time soon.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This idea is mainly based on the MLFQ, which has been proven effective for balancing response / turnaround time. To maintain this system, I have reviews each day and week (tracking with habitify) to make sure I stay consistent.

html:ul.html:li: For each work stream,
If I finished the allocated task for the day, get new task from priority 2.
Else, do nothing and continue with the current task.
Review inbox and move new tasks to corresponding work stream for review on Friday.
If immediately important, can move directly to priority 1 / 2.
Look at calendar for next day and timeblock work streams for the next day.
Since PhD work is so hard to predict, I have found timeblocking any further than one day in advance to be a futile effort.
Typically done on Fridays, so that I can dynamically allocate time on weekends if needed.
For each work stream,

html:ol.html:li: Look in remaining priority 2 and priority 3 tasks.
Define what I think a successful week would look like (e.g. if I get all these things done next week I will be happy).

html:ul.html:li: Look at calendar for week to guide this decision.

html:ol.html:li: Get rid of at least two of those tasks (initial plan always too ambitious).
Move / keep remaining tasks in priority 2.

html:ul.html:li: Write Weeknotes for the week.

fr:title.text: What to do when actually working on a task

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What to do when actually working on a task

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is the harder part (and requires the most discipline). When working on a task, especially a more open-ended one like research, it is very common that a million other things come up that seem immediately important. All of the sudden, you have a great idea for the research project you haven't worked on in months.

It is crucial to collect and forget about these things until they are scheduled. This is the one thing I did take from Getting Things Done. The system should flow like water. Making this achievable is where the todoist API becomes crucial.
I have a script bound by dwm that pops up vim for me to write down the task and, on save, it sends it to my inbox in todoist.
I have more involved ones that allow me to actually allocate it to the specific work stream / priority if it is immediately obvious. Another thing that I have found useful to actually achieving this watery flow is the pomodoro technique. This basically lets me gaslight myself into thinking I can go to the other things as soon as the timer ends (which is intentionally always just close enough). By the end, I often no longer have this urge and can continue on the scheduled tasks.

Due to the unstructured nature of PhD life / knowledge work in general, I have been trying to come up with the most effective system to ensure I am Getting Things Done (ironically I found this system actually not that helpful).

Since Cal Newport is a professor (can relate to the knowledge work problem), I have been reading a lot of his stuff on productivity.
Deep work.
Slow productivity.

I often refer back to Atomic Habits as it intuitively just makes so much sense to me.
Start small: starting a habit should take less than 2 minutes.
Never miss a habit twice.
Habit anchors: I am always going to run so now after I run I stretch, which has resulted in remarkable stretching consistency (see my Weeknotes).

fr:title.text: What to do when actually working on a task

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What to do when actually working on a task

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is the harder part (and requires the most discipline). When working on a task, especially a more open-ended one like research, it is very common that a million other things come up that seem immediately important. All of the sudden, you have a great idea for the research project you haven't worked on in months.

It is crucial to collect and forget about these things until they are scheduled. This is the one thing I did take from Getting Things Done. The system should flow like water. Making this achievable is where the todoist API becomes crucial.
I have a script bound by dwm that pops up vim for me to write down the task and, on save, it sends it to my inbox in todoist.
I have more involved ones that allow me to actually allocate it to the specific work stream / priority if it is immediately obvious. Another thing that I have found useful to actually achieving this watery flow is the pomodoro technique. This basically lets me gaslight myself into thinking I can go to the other things as soon as the timer ends (which is intentionally always just close enough). By the end, I often no longer have this urge and can continue on the scheduled tasks.
Rapidly exploring random trees (RRT) is a very simple, elegant and widely used algorithm for motion planning. The algorithm proceeds as follows:
fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Algorithm. For k = 1 to K do
x_{rand} \gets \mathsf {RANDOM\_STATE}()
x_{near} \gets \mathsf {NEAREST\_NEIGHBOR}(x_{rand}, \mathcal {T})
u \gets \mathsf {SELECT\_INPUT}(x_{near})
x_{new} \gets \mathsf {NEW\_STATE}(x_{near}, u, \Delta t)
\mathcal {T}.add\_vertex(x_{new})
\mathcal {T}.add\_edge((x_{near}, u, x_{new})) return \mathcal {T}

Remark. Given some dynamics model \dot {x} = f(x, u), \mathsf {NEW\_STATE} is some approximate integration, such as Euler integration i.e. x_{new} = x_{near} + f(x_{near}, u) \Delta t Preferable to use higher-order method like Runge-Kutta.

Remark. \mathsf {SELECT\_INPUT} is determined by whether the system is holonomic or non-holomonic. If the agent is holonomic, then u will just be the control corresponding to the difference in the two states.

fr:link.title: Deepseek v1 through R1: RL is back!

html:li.fr:link: Deepseek v1 through R1: RL is back!

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Request more below!
Notes on the paper Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head is All You Need.

For,
b batch dimension,
n sequence length,
d hidden dimension,
h number of heads. The total FLOPS for attention are roughly bnd^2. This comes from
3nd^2 FLOPS to compute Q = XW_Q, K = XW_K, V = XW_V.
Need to do this b times for each input in batch. and the total memory accesses are roughly \underbrace {bnd}_{X} + \underbrace {bhn^2}_{\text {softmax}} + \underbrace {d^2}_{\text {projection}}. This gives high arithmetic intensity O\left (\left (\frac {h}{d} + \frac {1}{bn}\right )^{-1}\right )

However, if we do incremental inference then we must multiply our total number of memory accesses by n i.e. bn^2d + nd^2. This gives arithmetic intensity O\left (\left (\frac {n}{d} + \frac {1}{b}\right )^{-1}\right ), which requires large batch and short sequence length.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. I believe we just ignore the softmax memory contribution in the inference case because it does not scale with n anymore as we are computing the logits just for the next token. Therefore, it becomes a \frac {d}{h} term, which we can safely ignore?
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The key is that the \frac {n}{d} term comes from the bn^2d term, where we are loading h (b \times n \times d / h) K and V matrices n times. Thus, we can improve the \frac {n}{d} term by a factor of h by simply not using a different K and V matrix for each head. This is the entire idea behind MQA.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Similar to the KV-cache, we still can use h Q matrices because we do not need to load n of them into memory because only the last one matters for inference.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Group query attention is the same idea as MQA but instead of using one K and V for every head, they use a group of them. Where obviously the number of groups needs to be less than the number of heads.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Grouped query uses subset of K and V matrices.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Also inspired by this idea of reducing the dependence of K and V on the sequence length is sliding window attention.

The idea behind sliding window attention is to ensure the number of weight matrices needed for the keys K and values V does not scale with the sequence length. Intuitively, this means allowing each word to "attend" to only some fixed number of previous words rather than the whole sequence

html:figure.html:figcaption: "the" only sees "on" and "sat" rather than the full sentence.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I often hear this question come up amongst performance-driven developers, and for the longest time I could never get a straight answer as to why the stack was so much better? After a long foray into the inner workings of how memory allocation is actually done, I think I finally have an answer. I do not want to forget this, so I am doing a quick write up here.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
This all comes down to what happens during each memory allocation. Prior to actually running the program, the operating system allocates some amount of memory for both the stack and the heap (I think around 2-4MB?). When you allocate memory on the stack i.e. int x = 1;
Stack just increments stack pointer.
Writes value to new address pointed to by stack pointer. When you allocate memory on the heap i.e. int *x = malloc(sizeof(int)), we must instead choose how best to fulfill the request, which is a whole research direction in itself.
One such implementation is the buddy algorithm However, malloc itself is not a system call and therefore is not terribly slow. A real issue occurs when we run out of pre-allocated heap memory. This requires invoking a system call like sbrk to increment the size of the allocated memory for the heap.
If no available pages, OS must evict and write to disk...
Formally, this is known as "heap growth". Generally, if you know how much memory you need it is good practice to just allocated all up front to minimize any potential system calls. With this, the performance difference is not too drastic :)

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This covers the math behind the online softmax algorithm required by Flash Attention (v1).
The first thing we must consider is numerical precision. Namely, the original softmax operation is given by
However, when operating with low precision data types (i.e. float16) the maximum value is 65536. Therefore, with \exp we can easily get numerically overflow.

To get around this, the online softmax algorithm adheres to the safe softmax. For safe softmax, we simply multiply the numerator and denominator by \exp (-m), where m = \max \{x_{1}, \ldots , x_n\}. This gives
Since x_i - m \leq 0, this ensures that each \exp (x_i - m) is bounded in a safe range.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Note that computing the \max over \mathbf {x} means that we must have all of the logits in a row of QK^T materialized in SMEM, or first loop over all of them to compute the max before computing the normalization constant.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The online softmax algorithm fuses the max reduction into the normalization pass, so a single sweep over the row suffices to compute the normalization with the max. The key difficulty is that the denominator depends on n for each i i.e.

Therefore, we must find a recursive relationship such that d_{i+1} = f(d_i, m_i, x_{i+1}) for some function f. This would then allow us to locally compute d_{i+1} with only the local information of current x_{i+1} and the two running statistics d_i and m_i. This is exactly the kind of algorithm that is conducive to memory tiling.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. What is f(d_i, m_i, x_{i+1})?
Answer. Consider d_{i+1} = d_i \cdot \exp (m_{i} - m_{i + 1}) + \exp (x_{i + 1} - m_{i + 1}).

Proof. This can be seen by directly rewriting d_{i + 1}.
\begin {align*} d_{i + 1} &= \sum _{j = 1}^{i+1} \exp (x_j - m_{i + 1}) \\ &= \left (\sum _{j = 1}^{i} \exp (x_j - m_{i+1})\right ) + \exp (x_{i+1} - m_{i+1}) \\ &= \left (\sum _{j = 1}^{i} \exp (x_j - m_{i})\right )\exp (m_{i} - m_{i + 1}) + \exp (x_{i+1} - m_{i+1}) \\ &= d_{i} \exp (m_{i} - m_{i + 1}) + \exp (x_{i+1} - m_{i+1}) \end {align*}
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The running denominator d is rescaled by e^{m - m_{\text {new}}} each time a new maximum is found, keeping it consistent with the safe-softmax shift.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Unfortunately, we cannot get around the second pass to compute the normalized output for \mathbf {p}. However, this is not the case in Flash Attention (v1).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Unlike in the online softmax, we are not interested in the outputted probability vector \mathbf {p}. Instead we are interested in the entire output matrix O given by
For more details see self-attention.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Notation. In correspondence with the notation in the online softmax, we define the following: x_{i,j} = \mathbf {q}_i \cdot \mathbf {k}_j, where \mathbf {q}_i is the ith row of Q and \mathbf {k}_j is the jth row of K. Similarly, \mathbf {o}_i is the ith row of the output matrix O given by \mathbf {o}_i = \sum _{j = 1}^{n} \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_n)}{d_n} \cdot \mathbf {v}_j
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Now following the template provided by the online softmax, we must find a recurrence for \mathbf {o}_i. Namely, we will consider the sequence \mathbf {o}_{i,k}' given by \mathbf {o}_{i,k}' = \sum _{j = 1}^{k} \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_j, such that \mathbf {o}_{i,n}' = \mathbf {o}_n
fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Claim. We have the following recurrence for \mathbf {o}_{i,k}':
\mathbf {o}_{i,k}' = \mathbf {o}_{i, k-1} \cdot \frac {d_{k - 1} \cdot \exp (m_{k-1} - m_{k})}{d_k} + \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_k

Proof. We will proceed by directly rewriting the recurrence for \mathbf {o}_{i,k}' as
\begin {align*} \mathbf {o}_{i,k}' &= \sum _{j = 1}^{k} \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_j \\ &= \left (\sum _{j = 1}^{k - 1} \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_{k-1})}{d_{k-1}} \cdot \mathbf {v}_j\right ) \frac {d_{k - 1} \cdot \exp (m_{k-1} - m_{k})}{d_k} + \frac {\exp (x_{i,k} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_k \\ &= \mathbf {o}_{i, k-1} \cdot \frac {d_{k - 1} \cdot \exp (m_{k-1} - m_{k})}{d_k} + \frac {\exp (x_{i,k} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_k .\end {align*}
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Thus, we are left with the following algorithm
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A key benefit of this algorithm is that it is entirely parallelizable across rows. This means that we can loop across entire tiles like in the GEMM case.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To understand the innovation of the backward pass in flash attention, we must first find all of the gradients we must compute.
We will compute each gradient individually.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. \mathbf {P} and d\mathbf {P} are N \times N matrices. Materializing this is equivalent to materialing the attention matrix in the forward pass.

Insight. recomputing \mathbf {P}_{ij} and d\mathbf {P}_{ij}.
First, we can easily recompute \mathbf {P}_{ij} with \mathbf {Q} and \mathbf {K} (which are both N \times d and must be resident in memory) via \mathbf {P}_{ij} = \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}}
We can apply the same trick to d\mathbf {P}_{ij} with d\mathbf {O} and \mathbf {V} (both N \times d). Namely, d\mathbf {P}_{ij} = \sum _{k} d\mathbf {O}_{ik} \mathbf {V}_{jk} = d\mathbf {O}_{i}^T \mathbf {V}_j
Never need an N \times N matrix.
Problem. \mathbf {D} requires computing the global dot product \mathbf {P}_i \cdot d\mathbf {P}_i.

Insight.
To get around this, we precompute \mathbf {D} using the \mathbf {O} values from the forward pass and the current d\mathbf {O} values. In particular,
\begin {align*} \mathbf {D}_i &= \mathbf {P}_{i} \cdot d\mathbf {P}_{i} \\ &= \sum _{j} \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} d\mathbf {P}_{ij} \\ &= \sum _{j} \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \mathbf {V}_{j} \\ &= \sum _{j} d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \left (\frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} \cdot \mathbf {V}_{j}\right ) \\ &= d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \sum _{j} \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} \cdot \mathbf {V}_{j} \\ &= d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \mathbf {O}_{i} .\end {align*}
Thus, we can compute \mathbf {D}_i directly from the vectors d\mathbf {O}_i, \mathbf {O}_i.

html:ul.html:li: In the forward pass,

html:ol.html:li: Compute and store \mathbf {O} values in memory.
Compute and store \mathbf {L} values in memory

html:ul.html:li: Between the forward and backward pass,

html:ol.html:li: Compute d\mathbf {O}. (This is done automatically in JAX)
Use \mathbf {O} and d\mathbf {O} to compute \mathbf {D}. O(n) memory.

html:ul.html:li: In the backward pass,

html:ol.html:li: Compute d\mathbf {V}_j via \mathbf {Q}, \mathbf {K}, d\mathbf {O}. O(d) memory.
Compute d\mathbf {Q}_i via \mathbf {Q}_i, \mathbf {K}_j, \mathbf {L}_i, \mathbf {D}_i. O(d) memory.
Compute d\mathbf {K}_j via \mathbf {Q}_i, \mathbf {K}_j, \mathbf {L}_i, \mathbf {D}_i. O(d) memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: As I grow more interested in abandoning batched updates and the replay buffer, the importance of elibility traces grows with me. Due to this, I thought it finally time to re-prove / understand what makes these traces so eligible.
The reference followed is Sutton and Barto (Ch 12.).

fr:title.text: Deepseek v1 through R1: RL is back!

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Deepseek v1 through R1: RL is back!

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In this blog, we will aim to understand the key contributions of DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in LLMs via Reinforcement Learning. It will serve as the complement to my group meeting presentation possibly consisting of more in-depth explanations. Time permitting, we might go over the engineering innovations introduced in DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report.

fr:title.text: Deepseek v1 through R1: RL is back! › How is R1 different then previous iterations of models?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Deepseek v1 through R1: RL is back! › How is R1 different then previous iterations of models?

html:ul.html:li: In R1-Zero, they do ZERO SFT on the base model - directly apply reinforcement learning.
Use PPO like policy optimization but do NOT learn a reward model.

Use very simple reward:
+1 for correct answer
-0.5 for incorrect answer
-1 for inability to answer.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Traditional actor critic RL algorithms, require training both an actor and a critic (as the name implies). Typically, these components are both of equal size. In the field of RL, this is non-problematic because models are typically rather small (at least in comparison to LLMs). In DeepSeekMath: Pushing the Limits of Mathematical Reasoning in Open Language Models

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
\begin {align*} {\mathcal {J}}_{\mathrm {GRPO}}(\theta )&= \mathbb {E}[q\sim P(Q),\{o_{i}\}_{i=1}^{G}\sim \pi _{\theta _{o l d}}(O|q)] \\ &= \frac {1}{G}\sum _{i=1}^{G}\left (\operatorname *{min}\left (\frac {\pi _{\theta }(o_{i}|q)}{\pi _{\theta _{o d}}(o_{i}|q)}A_{i},\operatorname *{clip}\left (\frac {\pi _{\theta }(o_{i}|q)}{\pi _{\theta _{o d d}}(o_{i}|q)},1-\varepsilon ,1+\varepsilon \right )A_{i}\right )-\beta \mathbb {D}_{K L}\left (\pi _{\theta }||\pi _{r e f}\right )\right ) \end {align*}
where
\mathbb {D}_{\mathrm {K L}}\left (\pi _{\theta }||\pi _{\mathrm {ref}}\right )=\frac {\pi _{\mathrm {ref}}(o_{i}|q)}{\pi _{\theta }(o_{i}|q)}-\log \frac {\pi _{\mathrm {ref}}(o_{i}|q)}{\pi _{\theta }(o_{i}|q)}-1
The astute RL reader will notice this is essentially PPO. The key distinction here is that the advantage A_i is not computed using a critic model. Instead, A_{i}=\frac {r_{i}-\mathrm {mean}(\{r_{1},r_{2},\cdots ,r_{G}\})}{\mathrm {std}(\{r_{1},r_{2},\cdots ,r_{G}\})}.

fr:title.text: Deepseek v1 through R1: RL is back! › Post-training

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Deepseek v1 through R1: RL is back! › Post-training

html:ul.html:li: Reinforcement Learning for all Scenarios: Seems like they do RLHF after the pure RL stage.
Do traditional helpfulness harmfulness RLHF with trained reward model.

fr:title.text: Deepseek v1 through R1: RL is back! › Distilling Models with R1

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Deepseek v1 through R1: RL is back! › Distilling Models with R1

html:ul.html:li: To distill, they do only SFT with R1 generated COT.
They show that distillation outperforms doing pure RL approach on smaller model

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I used LazyVim for a long time, but I kept hitting a wall: understanding how LazyVim did something was consistently harder than if I had just written the thing myself. So I gave myself one day to rebuild from scratch, with the modest goal of first-class support for \TeX , Python, Forester, and C/C++. That one-day sprint is long past, and the config has kept evolving since — this note describes where it landed, not the frantic first pass. The through-line is that almost everything now leans on Neovim's own primitives instead of a framework, which is exactly the transparency I was missing.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The whole config lives in my dotfiles. It is a thin init.lua that loads four modules — config.lazy, config.options, config.keymaps, and core.lsp — with the actual plugin specs under lua/plugins/ and per-server LSP tables under lsp/.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To my surprise, a lot of the behavior I had taken for granted turned out to be options LazyVim set internally. I was greeted on first launch by 8-space indents and a clipboard that would not survive a trip between terminal instances. So the first job was archaeology: I dug through LazyVim for every option I actually liked and copied them into config/options.lua. System clipboard, sensible folding via Treesitter (foldexpr = v:lua.vim.treesitter.foldexpr()), a global statusline, conceallevel for markup — the unglamorous foundation everything else sits on.

The plugin manager is lazy.nvim, the de facto standard, and it genuinely just works. The bootstrap snippet from their docs clones it on first run, and after that every file under lua/plugins/ that returns a spec table is imported automatically. No central manifest to keep in sync — a new file is a new plugin.

This is where the config diverged most sharply from where it started. The first pass used mason.nvim, mason-lspconfig, and nvim-lspconfig — the standard stack. All three are now gone. Neovim 0.11 ships a native LSP configuration API: vim.lsp.config defines a server and vim.lsp.enable turns it on, and any table returned from lsp/<name>.lua on the runtime path is picked up as that server's config. So core.lsp is just a list —

— and each server gets a plain Lua file in lsp/. No package manager, no bridge plugin translating between two config schemas. I install the servers themselves through my system package manager and let Neovim wire them up. It is dramatically less machinery for the same result, and when something breaks there is exactly one place to look.

The Python setup is the clearest upgrade. I dropped pyright for basedpyright and split responsibilities: basedpyright does type checking and inlay hints, while ruff runs as its own language server handling import organization and diagnostics. In the LazyVim days I fought constantly with import resolution, hand-writing a function to locate each project's virtualenv and pin pythonPath to its interpreter. basedpyright resolves environments well enough on its own that the hack is gone. clangd, texlab, and lua_ls round out the set, each configured in its own file — texlab in particular carries a fair amount of custom command wiring for building, forward search into Zathura, and environment manipulation.

A shared LspAttach autocommand in core.lsp binds the buffer-local LSP keymaps once, for every server: go-to-definition and friends route through fzf-lua pickers, and document highlighting, inlay-hint toggling, and native completion get enabled per-buffer based on what each client actually supports.

fr:title.text: Forester finally has a real language server

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Forester finally has a real language server

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The original post described an ugly hack to get Forester completion. The forester.nvim plugin exposed a completion source, but only for nvim-cmp; blink.cmp (which I use — see below) needs a blink.compat shim, and that shim expected a plugin to be a source rather than to bury one inside a larger feature set. Getting reference completion working meant reading blink.compat internals and registering the source by hand.

None of that survives, because Forester now ships an actual language server. My lsp/forester.lua just launches forester lsp and hands it the forest root. The one wrinkle worth recording: forester locates the forest by its process working directory and ignores the LSP rootUri, and Neovim does not spawn the server in root_dir by default. So the config resolves the nearest forest.toml and starts the RPC process with an explicit cwd — otherwise the server exits with status 1 whenever Neovim's cwd has no forest.toml (e.g. when I open a tree from $HOME). Reference completion — indispensable for writing this very blog — now flows through the standard LSP path with no shim in sight.

Completion is blink.cmp, the successor that finally displaced nvim-cmp. Its sources are lsp, path, snippets, and omni, with LuaSnip as the snippet engine. Because I lean on native LSP, the on_attach handler calls vim.lsp.completion.enable with autotrigger, so the server's completions feed the picker directly.

Layered on top is supermaven-nvim for AI ghost-text suggestions — accept-suggestion, accept-word, and clear bound to <C-l>, <C-j>, and <C-]>. It fills the role Copilot-style tools play elsewhere without getting in the way of the structured LSP and snippet completions.

Going all the way back to the Gilles Castel blog post, I have always wanted snippets that auto-expand. Under LazyVim I had settled for friendly-snippets on native Neovim snippets; rebuilding from scratch, I refused to compromise. The engine is LuaSnip, which blink.cmp supports natively, and I have to plug the luasnip-latex-snippets repo that ports the original UltiSnips math snippets over. My own snippets live in a small local plugin, custom-luasnip-snippets.nvim, vendored under lua/local/.

The trick I had wanted for years and can finally do: load LaTeX math snippets while inside math environments in Forester. The snippet plugin runs with use_treesitter = true, so it inspects the Forester Treesitter grammar, finds the node types for math environments, and only expands the math snippets when the cursor is actually inside one. Writing \TeX inside a .tree file feels exactly like writing it in a .tex file.

Formatting runs through conform.nvim on save. Python is handled by ruff_format followed by pyink, Lua by stylua — this replaced the old black + isort pairing, folding most of it into the same ruff that already runs as a language server. Formatting falls back to the LSP formatter when no dedicated tool is configured for a filetype.

The fuzzy finder is fzf-lua, which replaced Telescope. File and grep pickers are scoped to a detected project root — a small utils.root module (lifted from LazyVim) walks LSP workspace folders, .git, and cwd to find it — with a dedicated tex override that restricts live-grep to \TeX source files.

The piece I am most fond of is forester-new, a local plugin that wires the editor into the forest's own note pipeline. It shells out to scripts/new so every tree gets the standard frontmatter and routing, then commands like :ForesterNew, :ForesterStub, :ForesterCitek, and :ForesterTransient handle the follow-through: creating a tree and inserting a \transclude, stubbing a concept and dropping a [[id]] link at the cursor, or — in visual mode — lifting a selection into a new tree and replacing it in place with the transclude. Stub-and-link without ever leaving the sentence, which is exactly the friction the forest workflow is supposed to remove.

I originally built a whole in-editor Anki workflow on top of AnkiConnect and a vendored anki.nvim. That has since moved out of the editor and into the forest itself: cards are just \taxon{Card} trees, and scripts/anki-sync mirrors them into Anki — creating the note on first sync and updating in place thereafter. Writing a card is now the same as writing any other tree, snippets and all, and the sync is one command away. This is an example of a card.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Note that the gradient is with respect to \theta , where \theta is just the third argument to \mathsf {ELBO} as defined above. Notably, \theta _t will show up elsewhere later but will NOT be backpropagated through.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The above motivates the expectation step of the EM algorithm. Namely how can we compute the lower bound expectation for the best possible q.

Recall (or review) from the discussion on ELBO that q(z) = p(z | x; \theta ) maximizes the ELBO for a given x and \theta . Therefore, we actually want to learn q_{\phi }(z, x, \theta ) \approx p(z | x; \theta ). To do so, we can simply maximize the ELBO (duh). Formally, \phi _{t+1} = \argmax _{\phi } \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q}\left [\log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta _t)}{q_{\phi }(Z, x, \theta _t)}\right ]
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. Now this is all cool theoretically but how TF do we actually maximize this thing?
Answer. It is highly dependent on the parametrization of p and q.

html:div.html:p:
We define the problem as
p(z) \sim N(0, 1)
p(x | z; \theta ) \sim N(\mu _{\theta }(z), \sigma _{\theta }(z))
q_{\phi }(z | x, \theta ) \sim N(\mu _{\phi }(x), \sigma _\phi (x)) where \mu _{\{\phi , \theta \}}, \sigma _{\{\phi , \theta \}} are parametrized neural networks. Then we can rewrite the ELBO as (ignoring constants)
\begin {align*} \mathsf {ELBO}(x, q, \theta ) &= \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q(\cdot \mid x, \theta )} \left [\log p(Z) - \log \frac {p(x \mid Z; \theta )}{q_{\phi }(Z, x, \theta )}\right ] \\ &= \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q(\cdot \mid x, \theta )} \left [-\frac {Z^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((Z - \mu _{\theta }(Z))^2 - (Z - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ] .\end {align*}

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. We need to sample realization z \sim q_{\phi }(\cdot |x, \theta ). So isn't our realization z a function of \phi . How would we optimize this?
Answer. The reparametrization trick!!

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
For q_{\phi }(\cdot | x) \sim N(\mu _{\phi }(x), \sigma _{\phi }(x)). The reparametrization trick allows us to differentate through a random sample by sampling from q_{\phi }(\cdot | x) via z(\phi ) = \mu _{\phi }(x) + \sigma _{\phi }(x) \cdot \epsilon , where \epsilon \sim N(0, 1). Then, for a given sample \epsilon , we can easily compute 
abla _{\phi } z(\phi ) = 
abla _{\phi } \mu _{\phi }(x) + 
abla _{\phi }\sigma _{\phi }(x) \cdot \epsilon

Now for a given sample x_i \sim \mathcal {D}, we can get a sample z_i(\phi ) \sim q(\cdot \mid x_i, \theta ) that is differentiable with respect to \phi . Using this, we can get the stochastic gradient estimate \nabla _q \widetilde {\mathsf {ELBO}}(x, q, \theta ) = \nabla _{\phi } \left [\frac {z_i(\phi )^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\theta }(z_i(\phi )))^2 - (z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ]

Finally, we have reached the maximization step. This step is fairly straightforward after all the progress we have made. Once we have a tight lower bound i.e. q(z) \approx p(z \mid x; \theta ) then we simply fix q and maximize the ELBO for \theta i.e. in the above example and algorithm we instead compute \nabla _\theta \widetilde {\mathsf {ELBO}}(x, q, \theta ) = \nabla _{\theta } \left [\frac {z_i(\phi )^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\theta }(z_i(\phi )))^2 - (z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ] Alternating between these steps yields the expectation-maximization algorithm the ML oldheads mythologize.

Setup. We are given a joint distribution p(X, Z; \theta ), where X and Z are random variables with their joint parametrized by \theta . We are also given a dataset \mathcal {D} containing realizations of only X i.e. Z is a latent random variable.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Goal. We want to learn p(x; \theta ).
fr:mainmatter.html:p: However, p(x; \theta ) = \int p(x, z; \theta ) \mathrm {d}z = \int p(x | z; \theta )p(z) \mathrm {d}z, which is computationally intractable for most continuous Z.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. Can we update \theta for a given realization of x and z?
Answer. Yes!!!
fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Motivation. Suppose we had some q(z) that we could sample realizations of Z from. Then we want to find some reasonable \ell _{\theta }, such that \theta ^* \approx \arg \max _{\theta } \mathbb {E}_{x, \sim p(x; \theta ^*), z \sim q(z)}[\ell _{\theta }(X, Z)]
Remark. q(z) can potentially (and will) depend on the sample x_i. This is OK because we are given x_i's, which we can then just pass to q to get the corresponding z_i.
Why is this the case? Because then (for well-behaved \ell _{\theta }) \theta _{t + 1} = \theta _{t} - \alpha \nabla _{\theta _{t}}\ell _{\theta _{t}}(x_i, z_i) \to \theta ^* i.e. SGD is a reasonable thing to do, where the gradient is for a single sample of x_i and z_i which we have readily available from our dataset \mathcal {D} and our distribution q that we can sample from. This idea comes up a lot in optimization, such as the \log trick in the original policy gradient.

Similar to the \log trick, the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) comes from a simple multiply and divide trick. Namely, \begin {align*} \log p(x; \theta ) &= \log \int p(x, z; \theta ) \mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log \int p(x, z; \theta ) \frac {q(z)}{q(z)} \mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} \\ &\geq \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} .\end {align*} The last inequality follows directly from Jensen's Inequality. However, note that this is only a lower bound and therefore not exactly what we want. For example, if we were to pick q \sim N(0, 1) and then optimize the ELBO for \theta we would not necessarily find a good \theta for the likelihood \log p(x; \theta ). Luckily, we can actually get the difference \log p(x; \theta ) - ELBO in closed form! Namely, \begin {align*} \log p(x; \theta ) - \mathrm {ELBO} &= \log p(x; \theta ) - \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} \\ &= \int \left (\log p(x; \theta ) - \log \frac {p(x, z; \theta )}{q(z)}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {p(x; \theta )q(z)}{p(x, z; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {p(x; \theta )q(z)}{p(z | x; \theta )p(x; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {q(z)}{p(z | x; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \mathrm {KL}\big (q(z) \mid \mid p(z | x; \theta )\big ) .\end {align*} Thus, to tighten the lower bound we can optimize q_{\phi }(z) to try to match p(z | x; \theta ). As a sanity check, lets make sure these match when q(z) = p(z \mid x; \theta ). \begin {align*} \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} &= \int \log \frac {p(x, z; \theta )}{q(z)}\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \int \log \frac {p(x; \theta )p(z \mid x; \theta )}{p(z \mid x; \theta )}\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \int \log p(x; \theta )\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log (x; \theta ) .\end {align*} You may be wondering how we use this algorithmically to find \theta ^*? This will be the main idea of expectation maximization. Feel free to check it out!

I have been using todoist and habitify. I decided on these tools primarily because of their API availability. I wanted to be able to add / complete / see outstanding tasks or habits for the day without the allure of everything else my browser has to offer. Also visualization for my Weeknotes.

html:ul.html:li: I had Claude write a little script to have my next tasks listed when clicked in my status bar, so I do not have to open the browser to check what I need to do today.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: It seems how to properly use these (todoist in particular) is the subject of much debate. Here, I will outline what I converged to / works for ME right now. I have broken down my system into a hierarchy:

html:ol.html:li: Higher level: how to actually choose what to do.
Lower level: how to actually do the chosen task.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is effectively a task management system.

Work streams. I took this name from my investment banker friends (sounds cool / important). Basically, these are groupings I have made and allocated time for each day. They roughly consist of the following:

html:ul.html:li: Research / work (4hrs first thing in the morning):
Active work towards new artifact or paper i.e. coding up ideas.
In the absence of ideas, studying existing relevant papers.
Deliberate practice (2hrs after run / lunch):
Name taken from Cal Newport.
As of writing this, a current example is spending ~2hrs a day writing / optimizing known kernels in CuTe / Pallas / NKI.
Basically, something I need to understand or master for research that is not novel.
Extraneous Study (30min - 1hr):
Things not directly related to research, but I am curious about.
I try to maintain study of related computer science fields. Primarily, studying a textbook in something like OS, computer architecture, etc.
Habits / daily upkeep (1hr normally end of day):
Mainly consists of reviewing Anki cards daily that come from extraneous study / deliberate practice.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To decide on the next task, I use a priority-based system:

html:ol.html:li: Things I will be working on today / until they are done. At most ONE thing per work stream.
The backlog of things to do next

html:ul.html:li: An index to find things to move to priority 1 for the next day
Keep relatively small to avoid the crippling nature of choice - roughly on the order of things I want done this week.

html:ol.html:li: A larger backlog of things - should be completed in the next month or so.
Things I am not working on right now, or have any need to be completed any time soon.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This idea is mainly based on the MLFQ, which has been proven effective for balancing response / turnaround time. To maintain this system, I have reviews each day and week (tracking with habitify) to make sure I stay consistent.

html:ul.html:li: For each work stream,
If I finished the allocated task for the day, get new task from priority 2.
Else, do nothing and continue with the current task.
Review inbox and move new tasks to corresponding work stream for review on Friday.
If immediately important, can move directly to priority 1 / 2.
Look at calendar for next day and timeblock work streams for the next day.
Since PhD work is so hard to predict, I have found timeblocking any further than one day in advance to be a futile effort.
Typically done on Fridays, so that I can dynamically allocate time on weekends if needed.
For each work stream,

html:ol.html:li: Look in remaining priority 2 and priority 3 tasks.
Define what I think a successful week would look like (e.g. if I get all these things done next week I will be happy).

html:ul.html:li: Look at calendar for week to guide this decision.

html:ol.html:li: Get rid of at least two of those tasks (initial plan always too ambitious).
Move / keep remaining tasks in priority 2.

html:ul.html:li: Write Weeknotes for the week.

fr:title.text: What to do when actually working on a task

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What to do when actually working on a task

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is the harder part (and requires the most discipline). When working on a task, especially a more open-ended one like research, it is very common that a million other things come up that seem immediately important. All of the sudden, you have a great idea for the research project you haven't worked on in months.

It is crucial to collect and forget about these things until they are scheduled. This is the one thing I did take from Getting Things Done. The system should flow like water. Making this achievable is where the todoist API becomes crucial.
I have a script bound by dwm that pops up vim for me to write down the task and, on save, it sends it to my inbox in todoist.
I have more involved ones that allow me to actually allocate it to the specific work stream / priority if it is immediately obvious. Another thing that I have found useful to actually achieving this watery flow is the pomodoro technique. This basically lets me gaslight myself into thinking I can go to the other things as soon as the timer ends (which is intentionally always just close enough). By the end, I often no longer have this urge and can continue on the scheduled tasks.

This was a very different read for me. A short book (only 135 pages) that goes into great detail the work day at the Mezzanine. I have some gripes with the description of readable on the cover. I found the incredibly vivid imagery sometimes hard to follow. It seems the intent of the book was to highlight the thoughts of an ordinary working man throughout the day. However, some of these thoughs were so far from ordinary it lost the relatable, funny read it was going for. Some chapters were pure gold (the urinal) but others were a huge miss for me.
Overall, I enjoyed the book and would recommend. 6.5/10.

html:figure.html:figcaption: One of many book covers. Not the best IMO.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A practical scheduler that approximates STCF scheduling's turnaround properties and Round-robin scheduling's response properties without requiring foreknowledge of job lengths. Maintain multiple priority queues; within a queue, schedule round-robin. Never service a lower-priority queue while a higher-priority queue has a runnable job.
The priority of a job is not assigned by the user — it is learned from behavior:

html:ul.html:li: New jobs enter at the top priority.
Each priority level has an allotment — the total CPU time a job may consume before being demoted.
Once a job exhausts its allotment at a level, it drops to the next level.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Short jobs that finish quickly never leave the top queue (good turnaround). Interactive jobs that yield often stay at the top (good response time). CPU-bound jobs sink to the bottom and get scheduled when nothing better is runnable.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Gaming the allotment: a job can issue I/O just before its allotment expires to avoid demotion, since I/O time doesn't count.
Solution. Charge the job's allotment for total time at the priority level, including time spent blocked.
Problem. Starvation of low-priority queues if higher queues never empty.
Solution. Periodically reset all jobs to the top priority. Cheap and effective.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Notes on the book Inside the Machine by Jon Stokes, restructured as a concept hierarchy.

The CPU's nearest tier of storage — the first NUMA level. Registers sit physically next to the ALUs and are addressed by name in the instruction encoding rather than by memory address. Their count is limited by the chip's surface area, which is why main memory exists at all.

The register file is the structure that holds the architecturally-visible registers. To support a superscalar machine, the file needs enough read and write ports to feed every ALU simultaneously: for an n-ALU chip with two operands per op, that's 2n read ports and n write ports.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Die area for a register file scales quadratically in the number of ports.
Solution. Split it. Different execution units (integer, floating-point, vector) get their own smaller register files. Fewer ports per file, faster access, less area.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Beyond the architecturally-named registers, modern chips keep many extra rename registers used by register renaming to break false data dependencies.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Instructions distinguish "immediate types" from "register types" so the decoder knows whether a field encodes a register name or a literal value.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The classical model of CPU operation: each instruction goes through three phases.

html:ol.html:li: Fetch: read the instruction from memory into the instruction register; increment the program counter.
Decode: extract the opcode, the operand register names, and the destination from the instruction register.
Execute: route to the appropriate functional unit — ALU for arithmetic, load/store unit for memory ops, branch unit for branches.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Instructions don't take arbitrary time. The motherboard's clock cycle bounds when latched values become visible to the next stage.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: pipeline decomposes this loop into independently-running pipeline stages so multiple instructions are in flight at once. Superscalar machines run multiple instructions per stage. Both are extensions of this same three-phase model.
An instruction that potentially changes the program counter to something other than "the next sequential instruction." Two flavors:

html:ol.html:li: Unconditional: the PC is set to the destination address, period.
Conditional: the PC is set to the destination only if some condition holds.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Conditional branches require the hardware to remember the outcome of recent arithmetic — typically in a processor status word (PSW) register, with one bit per condition (zero, sign, carry, overflow). Each conditional-branch variant tests a particular PSW bit.

In a pipelined CPU, branches stall the front-end: until the branch resolves, the fetcher doesn't know what to fetch next. This is the control hazard, and modern machines mitigate it with Speculative execution driven by Branch prediction.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. If the program uses relative addressing, the OS supplies a code-segment base to add to the branch destination.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The fixed series of steps a machine performs from power-on until a usable operating system is running. Each stage runs from a known location and hands off control to the next.

html:ol.html:li: Power signal resets the CPU to a known state.
The CPU fetches from a hard-wired address — the BIOS (or UEFI firmware). The BIOS runs basic hardware checks (POST).
The BIOS loads and jumps to the bootloader (e.g. GRUB) from a known location on disk.
The bootloader places the kernel image at the correct address and jumps to its entry point.
The kernel performs early setup: installs the Trap table (a special privileged instruction), starts the timer interrupt, sets up memory management, and finally enters user space.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The hardware is hard-wired only to find the BIOS. Everything past that is software the user can replace, which is why dual-booting and custom bootloaders work.

fr:title.text: Pipelining overlaps instruction stages to retire one per cycle

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Pipelining overlaps instruction stages to retire one per cycle

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Decomposing the Fetch-execute loop into independently-running stages so that multiple instructions are in flight simultaneously. A classical four-stage pipeline:

html:ol.html:li: Fetch from memory.
Decode.
Execute (the original execute phase, minus the write).
Write the result back to register or memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each stage runs concurrently on different instructions. The clock cycle is bounded by the slowest stage, not the whole loop — so dividing the work into roughly-equal-length stages is essential. With n stages running in steady state, the machine retires one instruction per cycle even though each instruction takes n cycles to traverse.

html:ol.html:li: Completion rate: instructions completed per nanosecond. Program time is \frac {\text {instructions per program}}{\text {avg completion rate}}.
Instruction throughput: instructions retired per clock cycle.
Instruction latency: cycles to complete one instruction (\geq the number of stages, often more in practice).

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Pipelining doesn't make any one instruction faster — it raises throughput. Pushing this further (deeper pipelines, more stages) shrinks the clock cycle but increases per-instruction latency and the cost of pipeline stalls or flushes.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: What stops the steady-state throughput of one instruction per cycle is hazards: data hazard, structural hazard, and control hazard. The next layer of complication is going superscalar — multiple instructions per stage — which is covered in superscalar.

fr:title.text: Superscalar CPUs issue multiple instructions per cycle

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Superscalar CPUs issue multiple instructions per cycle

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Multiple Execution unitss on the same chip running in parallel during the same pipeline stage. Decode becomes decode/dispatch: the front-end must decide which instructions can issue in parallel and route them to the right units. Going superscalar amplifies the cost of every pipeline data hazard and control hazard, which is why the rest of this chapter's machinery — Forwarding, register renaming, Branch prediction — exists.
The functional circuitry that actually performs an instruction. A superscalar CPU contains several, dispatched to in parallel during the execute stage of the pipeline. The common families:

html:ul.html:li: ALU — arithmetic logic unit, performs arithmetic (add, sub, mul, div) and logical (AND, OR, NOT) operations on integers and on special registers like the PSW. Typically split into integer logic units (ILU) and floating-point units (FPU).
Load-store unit — performs memory loads and stores. Carries a small address-calculation adder for address-mode resolution.
Branch execution unit — evaluates branch conditions (often by reading the PSW) and signals whether the branch is taken.
Vector / SIMD units — operate on wide registers (SSE, AVX, AltiVec) for data-parallel work.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Per the convention in Inside the Machine, "ALU" often refers to any execution unit for arithmetic or logical operations — including integer logic units (ILU) and floating-point units (FPU).
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each unit's reads and writes flow through a Register file. Splitting the register file per-unit reduces port count and access time.

The internal organization of a CPU that implements a given instruction set architecture (ISA). Two chips can present identical ISAs to software while having radically different microarchitectures — e.g. Intel and AMD both implement x86, but their pipelines, branch predictors, and execution units differ.

The bridge between the two is the microcode engine: a mini-CPU inside the CPU. It holds small programs in a microcode ROM and runs them when a complex instruction needs to be translated into the simpler primitives the hardware actually executes. Conceptually, it's an emulator.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. CISC vs RISC-style ISAs need large microcode engines to implement their complex instructions. RISC designs minimize the microcode engine, pushing translation to the compiler instead.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The translated primitives are Micro-operations — a finer-grained, more uniform instruction stream that the actual hardware schedules. The translation step is the main reason an x86 chip's pipeline starts with such complex decode logic.

fr:title.text: Data hazards stall when an instruction needs a not-yet-written value

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Data hazards stall when an instruction needs a not-yet-written value

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When one instruction's result is the next instruction's input, a naive pipeline would have to stall: the second instruction can't execute until the first has written back. Data hazards are the most pervasive source of stalls in a CPU.
Two standard mitigations:

html:ul.html:li: Forwarding — route the producer's output directly into the consumer's input, before the writeback stage commits it. The consumer sees the result one cycle earlier.

register renaming — false dependencies, where two instructions write to the same architectural register but operate on independent values, can be broken by mapping the architectural register to two different physical registers.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A data hazard mitigation: the producing instruction's result is routed from the output of the execute stage directly to the input of the next instruction's execute stage, bypassing the register-file write and read. The dependent consumer can execute one cycle later instead of waiting for writeback.
Conceptually, the pipeline grows wiring rather than stages. The result is "forwarded" from where it appears to where it's needed.

Register renaming is the data hazard mitigation that makes out-of-order execution viable: the hardware dynamically remaps each architectural-register write to a fresh physical register from a larger pool, so two instructions that reuse the same architectural name for independent values never collide. The false data dependency — same architectural register, unrelated values — simply disappears, because each write lands in a different physical register.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. Consider: add r1, r2, r3 // r2 := r1 + r3 add r4, r5, r2 // r2 := r4 + r5 (reuses r2 for an independent value) The second add only depends on r2 as a destination, not as a source. Without renaming, it would have to wait for the first instruction's writeback to avoid clobbering. With renaming, the second write goes to a fresh physical register, and the two instructions can proceed in parallel.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The programming model exposes only the architecturally-named registers; the Register file hides a larger pool of rename registers underneath. This is one of the central tricks that makes out-of-order execution viable.

fr:title.text: What false dependencies does register renaming eliminate?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What false dependencies does register renaming eliminate?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Write-after-write (\mathrm {WAW}) and write-after-read (\mathrm {WAR}) hazards — the false dependencies that arise from reusing an architectural register, not from real data flow. Removing them exposes more Register renaming breaks false dependencies to the scheduler.

fr:title.text: Structural hazards stall when instructions contend for one resource

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Structural hazards stall when instructions contend for one resource

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two simultaneous instructions need the same hardware resource, and there's only one of it. The classic example is a single-port Register file facing two writes in one cycle, or two memory-accessing instructions facing a single load-store unit.
Mitigations cost area:

html:ul.html:li: Duplicate the resource (extra ALU, more register-file write ports).
Pipeline the resource (multi-cycle dividers that accept a new input each cycle).
Stall — accept the cost in throughput.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Register file area scales quadratically in port count, so duplicating ports for many ALUs gets expensive quickly.
Solution. Per-unit register files (one for integer, one for FP, one for vector) with fewer ports each. Discussed under Register file.

fr:title.text: Control hazards stall fetch until the branch resolves

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Control hazards stall fetch until the branch resolves

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When the CPU reaches a Branch instruction in the pipeline, it doesn't yet know what to fetch next. Until the branch's condition resolves and its target is computed — several stages later — fetch is stuck.
Mitigations:

html:ul.html:li: Branch prediction — guess where control flow is going.
Speculative execution — actually run instructions down the predicted path, ready to throw the work away if the guess was wrong.
Instruction caching — keep recently-fetched instructions handy so the cost of restarting fetch on a mispredict is at least bounded by cache latency, not DRAM latency.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The cost of a mispredicted branch is roughly the pipeline depth — every speculatively-issued instruction has to be squashed and the pipeline refilled. Deeper pipelines amplify this cost, which is one reason Pentium 4 vs G4e: design philosophies (deep pipeline, narrow & deep) and superscalar-style designs (wider, shallower) make different tradeoffs.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The mechanism that lets the front-end of a pipelined CPU keep fetching past a Branch instruction before the branch has actually resolved. The fetcher consults a predictor; if the prediction is right, the pipeline runs at full throughput; if wrong, the speculatively-issued instructions are squashed.

Program-agnostic rules: e.g. "always predict taken", "always predict not-taken", or "predict backward branches taken, forward branches not-taken". Cheap, but accuracy is poor outside of loop-heavy code.
Driven by per-branch history kept in two hardware tables:

html:ul.html:li: Branch history table (BHT) — indexed by branch address, holds a small counter (typically 2-bit saturating) tracking how often the branch was taken.
Branch target buffer (BTB) — holds the target address each branch was observed to jump to. A BTB miss is expensive: even if you correctly predict "taken", you still don't know where to fetch from.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Per-branch prediction allows a counter to learn pattern-specific behavior — e.g. a loop counter takes the back-edge N - 1 times then falls through.

The Pentium famously combined the two: static prediction on a BHT miss, dynamic prediction otherwise. Later machines added refinements — PowerPC 750 added a Trace cache-like branch target instruction cache (BTIC) that stores the first few instructions at the target, eliminating the re-fetch latency. The Pentium M added a loop detector for loops that always iterate the same number of times.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. See Speculative execution for what happens once a prediction has been made.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Run instructions before knowing whether they should actually be run, on the assumption that you'll usually be right. Used after Branch prediction to keep the pipeline busy past a Branch instruction: instructions on the predicted path enter the pipeline, execute, and stash their results in non-architectural state. Only when the branch resolves are speculated results "committed" to architectural state — or, if the prediction was wrong, discarded.
Speculative state lives in the Reorder buffer (and the register renaming rename registers). Entries are tagged as speculative; on misprediction the speculative entries are flushed.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Modern speculative execution drives a lot of CPU complexity — and a lot of CPU security work. Spectre-class side channels exploit the fact that speculative execution can leave traces in caches even after the speculative results are discarded.

fr:title.text: Out-of-order execution runs ready instructions past a stalled one, committing in program order

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Out-of-order execution runs ready instructions past a stalled one, committing in program order

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Execute instructions in whatever order their operands become available, then commit them back in program order. The programmer's model still sees sequential execution; the hardware reorders aggressively underneath to keep Execution units busy across data hazards and memory latency.
Two structures make this work:

html:ul.html:li: Reservation stations — hold instructions waiting on operands, so a stalled instruction doesn't block independent ones behind it.
Reorder buffer — preserves program order so commits look sequential and mispredicted Speculative execution paths can be unwound.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Combined with register renaming to break false dependencies, the back-end of a modern CPU is essentially a dynamic scheduler that uses the program order only as a hint about what to retire next.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. An out-of-order pipeline can issue a load early (because its address operands are ready), execute several unrelated ALU ops while waiting for the memory, then have all four instructions ready to commit in program order. The illusion of sequential execution is preserved; the parallelism is real.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The structure that lets an out-of-order execution CPU look in-order to the programmer. The ROB holds every in-flight instruction in original program order, regardless of the order they actually executed. When the oldest entry has its result ready and all earlier entries have already committed, the ROB commits it — writing its result to architectural state and freeing the slot.

For stores, commit is only half the story. The ROB entry is freed and the instruction is architecturally real, but the value has not yet reached the cache — it is handed off to the Store buffer for asynchronous drain. This is what keeps the ROB from stalling behind every cache miss: once a store is known to be non-speculative, the front-end can move on.
Each ROB entry typically tracks:

html:ul.html:li: Instruction identity and program order position.
Operand register names (and renamed sources).
Status — pending, executing, complete, speculated.
The instruction's result, held in a data field that doubles as a register renaming register.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Speculatively-issued instructions also enter the ROB but are tagged. On a branch misprediction, all speculative entries past the mispredicted branch are squashed.

The Pentium Pro introduced this design with a 40-entry ROB. The ROB sits at both ends of the pipeline — dispatch enters it, retirement leaves it — which is what makes the rest of the back-end free to issue and execute out of order.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. ROB size, Reservation station count, and pipeline depth set each other's bounds. A shallow pipeline can afford a small ROB; a deep one (Pentium 4 had 126) needs a large one to keep the back-end fed.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A buffer in front of an Execution units (or group of them) that holds dispatched instructions waiting on their operands. Once all operands arrive — either by writeback or by Forwarding — the instruction is issued to its execution unit. Reservation stations exist so that an instruction waiting on a slow operand doesn't block dispatch of unrelated instructions behind it.
Two organizational choices:

html:ul.html:li: One reservation station per execution unit (Pentium Pro). Smaller, simpler.
One shared station feeding multiple units (also seen on Pentium Pro, where it issues up to five micro-ops per cycle).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Within a single station, instructions issue in FIFO order. Across stations, there is no guarantee — that's what out-of-order execution actually means.
Dispatching an instruction into a reservation station has four prerequisites:

html:ol.html:li: In-order rule: instructions dispatch in program order (they execute out of order, not dispatch).
Execution unit availability: the matching reservation station has room.
Completion buffer availability: the Reorder buffer has room to track the new in-flight instruction.
Register-rename availability: enough rename registers exist to hold the new instruction's destination.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The mechanism by which a modern out-of-order CPU notices that a previously-stalled instruction now has all its operands ready. The issue queue holds the N in-flight instructions whose operands are not yet available; each entry watches a small set of physical register tags (the operands it is waiting for). When a producer finishes, it broadcasts "tag T is ready" to every entry, and each entry compares that tag against its own waiting tags through a content-addressable memory (CAM). Entries whose last operand arrives become eligible to issue this cycle; a separate select stage picks which of the now-ready entries actually fires.

The cost is the reason CPU issue queues are small. Each entry runs a CAM lookup against each broadcast tag, so an N-entry queue with N producers per cycle scales as O(N^2) in comparators, wires, and power. The select stage compounds this — priority encoders across N entries are themselves a quadratic-area structure on the critical timing path. The cumulative wakeup-select latency is what bounds clock frequency once N grows past a few tens.

This is the structural reason CPU out-of-order windows stay in the tens-to-low-hundreds of entries even as transistor budgets grow. Wider doesn't help: timing closure on the CAM degrades faster than the additional ILP repays. GPUs sidestep the whole problem by leaning on Thread-level parallelism — a cheap per-register scoreboard plus a warp scheduler covers stalls without any broadcast/compare structure.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Wakeup-select sits at the boundary between Reservation station (which holds the instruction) and register renaming (which named the tags being broadcast). All three structures together are what people mean by "the out-of-order back-end."

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two design philosophies for an instruction set architecture.

html:ul.html:li: Complex instruction set computing (CISC) — many specialized instructions, including ones that combine memory access with arithmetic or operate on complex types (strings, decimal numbers). One instruction often does the work of several. x86 is the canonical example.

Reduced instruction set computing (RISC) — a small set of simple, uniform instructions; explicit load and store; arithmetic only on registers. Each instruction does little but maps cleanly to hardware. PowerPC, MIPS, ARM are RISC.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. CISC needs a fat Microarchitecture engine to translate its complex instructions into hardware-executable primitives. That's silicon area, power, and design complexity that doesn't directly add throughput.

Solution. Once RISC clearly won as a hardware model, x86 vendors adopted instruction translation: a CISC front-end decodes x86 into RISC-like Micro-operations internally. The chip is RISC on the inside, CISC on the outside.

Remark. The cost of legacy is real. The original Pentium spent roughly 30% of its transistors on legacy support (mostly the microcode ROM). The Pentium Pro spent 40% on backward compatibility. Modern x86 chips have absorbed this overhead because they can — transistor budgets grew faster than the legacy tax.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The hardware-internal instruction stream that an x86 chip actually schedules. Each user-visible x86 instruction is decoded into one or more micro-ops, which are simpler, more uniform, RISC-like primitives that the back-end can execute, reorder, and rename freely.

Many common x86 instructions translate to just one or two micro-ops, so decoders are split into simple and complex variants — the simple decoders are specific to single-micro-op instructions and run in parallel with the complex one.
Optimizations that operate on the micro-op stream:

html:ul.html:li: Micro-op fusion (Pentium M) — two related micro-ops that the back-end tracks together while sharing a slot in the issue queue. A store decomposes into a store-address and store-data pair, which are fused; a read-modify decomposes into a load + ALU op that fuse and execute serially.

Macro fusion (Core 2 Duo) — fuse a pair of x86 instructions (e.g. compare + branch) before decode, so a single micro-op represents both. Increases peak decode bandwidth without growing ROB or RS pressure.
Trace cache (Pentium 4) — cache decoded micro-op sequences for hot code so decoding doesn't have to happen on every iteration.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Micro-ops exist because CISC vs RISC-style ISAs need to be translated to something the hardware can schedule cleanly. Pure RISC chips don't need them.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An L1-level cache of decoded Micro-operations sequences. Instead of caching raw x86 instructions that have to be decoded on every fetch, a trace cache stores micro-op traces ready for the back-end. Introduced in the Pentium 4 to keep its very deep pipeline fed when normal x86 decoding couldn't run fast enough.
The trace cache operates in two modes:

html:ul.html:li: Execution mode: on a trace hit, feed the cached micro-ops directly to the back-end. Decode is bypassed entirely.
Build mode: on a trace miss, fetch the instructions from L2, decode them, and assemble a new trace while feeding it forward.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The trace cache is indexed during Branch prediction, so speculative paths beyond a branch can be stored in the same trace and fed forward without restarting fetch. This lets a single cache line hold six micro-ops that may include a speculated branch target.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Long x86 instructions that decode to hundreds of micro-ops would blow out the trace cache.
Solution. Tag those instructions. When the trace cache encounters a tag, it stores a pointer to the microcode ROM rather than the micro-ops themselves, and the ROM streams them on demand.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The CPU clock is decreasing much faster than main-memory latency, so without a cache hierarchy a fast CPU spends most of its time stalled on memory.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. If you are a really fast factory, the size of the truck doesn't matter — you must still wait the full duration of the first delivery.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Multiple tiers of progressively-larger, progressively-slower storage between the CPU's registers and main memory. Each tier holds the most-recently-used contents of the next slower tier, exploiting Spatial locality and Temporal locality in the workload.
Approximate tiers on a modern x86 chip:

html:ul.html:li: Registers — ns, dozens of bytes per core.
L1 cache — 2–8 ns, 8–128 KB per core. Often split into instruction and data L1s.
Built from static RAM (4–6 transistors per cell), which is fast but area-expensive vs. DRAM's single-transistor cell.
L2 cache — 5–12 ns, 0.5–8 MB. Closes the latency gap between L1 and DRAM.
L3 cache — slower, shared across cores.
Main memory — 10–60 ns, GBs.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. A byte's journey. L1 checked first; on miss, L2; on miss, main memory; on miss in main memory, Page fault to disk.

Remark. L1 is split into instruction and data caches because the locality profiles of code and data are very different — re-running the same code over different data has high temporal locality for code but low for data.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A cache miss happens when the requested data isn't in cache and has to be fetched from a slower tier. Misses fall into four categories — the "four Cs" of cache misses:

html:ol.html:li: Compulsory miss — the data was never in cache. Cold-start misses are unavoidable for a given cache size and working set.
Conflict miss — the data was evicted because of an associativity collision. A different access mapped to the same set kicked it out. Reduced by higher Cache associativity.
Coherence miss — the data was invalidated because another core wrote to it. Specific to shared-memory multicore systems.
Capacity miss — the cache filled up and the data was evicted by Cache replacement policy. The working set doesn't fit.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Distinguishing them matters because they have different mitigations: conflict misses respond to associativity changes, capacity misses respond to size, coherence misses respond to cache-coherence protocol design, and compulsory misses respond to prefetching.

The empirical observation that if a program accesses memory address x, it is likely to access addresses near x soon. Sequential array traversal, struct field access, and instruction fetch all exhibit it.

Caches exploit spatial locality by loading whole cache lines (typically 64 bytes) on every miss, not just the single requested byte. The byte that triggered the load is the critical word; the surrounding bytes come along for free under the assumption you'll want them.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. RAM and caches are organized in cache-line granularity throughout. Bandwidth is sized for whole-line transfers; tags are per-line, not per-byte.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The complement is Temporal locality — repeated access to the same data over time. Temporal locality implies spatial locality (you re-touch nearby bytes when you re-touch the original), but not vice versa.
If a program accesses memory address x, it is likely to access x again soon. Loop variables, frequently-called functions, and re-used data structures all exhibit it.

Caches exploit temporal locality by retaining recently-accessed lines until something more important takes their slot. The Cache replacement policy is what decides "more important" — typically LRU and the clock algorithm-ish.
Temporal locality implies spatial locality (re-touching a byte tends to re-touch its neighbors) but not vice versa: a sequential scan has spatial locality with no temporal locality.
The rule that decides which slots in the cache an address is allowed to occupy. Three points on the spectrum:

html:ul.html:li: Direct mapped — every address maps to exactly one slot. Fast (only one tag to check) but suffers conflict misses: two hot addresses that map to the same slot evict each other.
Fully associative — any address can live in any slot. No conflict misses but tag check is expensive (compare against every entry in parallel).

n-way set associative — middle ground. The cache is divided into sets of n slots; an address maps to a specific set but can occupy any of its n slots. The hot setting on real caches is somewhere between 4-way and 16-way.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Higher associativity reduces conflict misses but increases tag-check hardware and power. The cache tag of an address is the bits that aren't used to pick the set — they're stored alongside the data and compared on every access.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The tag array is stored separately from the data array (tag RAM) so the tag comparison can happen in parallel with the data lookup. Tags are small, allowing many in-flight comparisons.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Within a Cache associativity set, when a new line arrives and the set is full, which existing line gets evicted? Same question as LRU and the clock algorithm for pages, different timescales.

The hot policy is least recently used — but pure LRU requires tracking a per-line ordering and updating it on every hit, which is too expensive at L1 latencies. Real caches use pseudo-LRU — a tree of single bits per set that approximates LRU with a fraction of the bookkeeping. The clock algorithm is one well-known pseudo-LRU scheme.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Block (cache line) size is part of the same tradeoff. Larger blocks improve Spatial locality but cause cache pollution (you fetch bytes you don't use) and eat memory bandwidth. The sweet spot is around 64 bytes on modern x86.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When a cached line is modified, when do you propagate the change to the next tier?

html:ul.html:li: Write-through — every store updates both the cache and main memory. Simple, but generates memory traffic on every write.

Write-back — stores update only the cache. The line is marked dirty via a per-line bit; the next-tier copy is updated only when the line is evicted. Less memory traffic but the cache is now the sole authoritative source until eviction, which complicates correctness.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Real CPUs run write-back at L1 and L2. Write-through is occasionally used for specific lines (e.g. memory-mapped device registers where every write must hit the device immediately).
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Write-back is one of the constraints behind cache-coherence protocols on multicore chips: every core must agree on which one currently owns a dirty line.
The hard limit on per-core clock speed that emerged in the early 2000s, ending the era of frequency scaling. Power dissipation has two components on a chip:

html:ul.html:li: Dynamic power density — watts per unit area dissipated by transistor switching. Scales with clock frequency: faster switching, more energy per second. You can also drive it up by cramming more transistors into the same area.
Static power density — watts per unit area dissipated by leakage current through "off" transistors. Smaller transistors leak more, so denser chips leak more even when idle.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: By the mid-2000s, single-core frequency scaling was thermally unsustainable. The industry pivoted to low-power design and Multicore — get more throughput from many slower cores instead of one faster one. Pentium M and the Core series were the first generations explicitly designed under this constraint.

Multiple independent CPU cores on the same chip. A core, for this purpose, is the bundle of front-end, back-end, Execution unitss, and L1 Cache hierarchy that runs one architectural thread of execution.

Traditional multiprocessor systems put each core on its own physical chip; chip multiprocessing (CMP) puts multiple cores on the same die. The key advantage is shared lower-level caches — typically an L2 or L3 shared across all cores on the chip — which speeds up inter-core communication and amortizes silicon.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The shift to multicore was forced by the Power wall more than chosen for its own merits. The software cost is real: shared-memory parallelism requires correctness work that single-threaded code didn't.
