fr:mainmatter.html:p: Multicore is what makes Concurrency a hardware problem, not just a scheduling problem: real simultaneous execution on different physical cores means software has to handle Race conditions with real synchronization primitives like Locks.

What the CPU does, entirely in hardware, the moment an interrupt arrives — whether a device IRQ, a fault like a Page fault, or a software int N. The point of the hardware path is to land in the kernel at a clean instruction boundary with enough saved state that user execution can resume later as if nothing happened.
Four things happen, in order, with no software involvement:

html:ol.html:li: Reach a clean instruction boundary. The CPU cannot pause halfway through, say, a memory write — architectural state would be inconsistent. So the current instruction either finishes normally (typical for asynchronous interrupts like a timer) or is rolled back as if it never started (faults, where the instruction couldn't complete anyway and needs to be retried). A page-fault handler maps in the missing page and UHNL returns to re-execute the faulting instruction; a timer handler returns to the next instruction.

Save the minimum resume state to the kernel stack. The CPU pushes RIP (where to resume), RSP (the user stack pointer it's about to abandon), the privilege level (CS — were we in ring 3 or ring 0?), and RFLAGS. The save target is the kernel stack, whose address the CPU reads from the Task state segment that the OS set up at boot. This is a security boundary: the kernel never trusts the user stack, since user code could have corrupted it or pointed RSP at something malicious. Note what is not saved: general-purpose registers. The handler itself pushes RAX, RBX, ... if it uses them, which keeps the hardware path fast.

Jump to the handler via the Interrupt descriptor table. The CPU takes the interrupt number N, indexes entry N of the IDT (found via the IDTR register), loads the handler address into RIP, and loads the new code segment, which switches the CPU from ring 3 to ring 0. After this step, the CPU is running kernel code on the kernel stack at the right privilege level — all set up automatically.

Flush the Reorder buffer of work past the interrupt point. In an out-of-order execution CPU the ROB may already contain instructions from after the interrupt — they've been issued speculatively because the front-end ran ahead. Those entries are discarded so the handler sees clean architectural state, as if execution had stopped exactly at the interrupt point. When UHNL returns, those instructions re-execute normally. This is the same squash machinery used for branch mispredictions in Speculative execution — and, famously, the reason Spectre-class side channels exist: speculative work leaves observable traces in caches even after the ROB is flushed.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Once the hardware path finishes, the OS's interrupt handler is running. Its first job is typically to save the remaining registers before doing real work — the rest is software.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Steps 2 and 3 are why an OS needs to set up the IDT and TSS before enabling interrupts. Until both structures point somewhere valid, the first interrupt would either jump to garbage or push to a user-controlled stack — both fatal.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The CPU's lookup table from interrupt number to handler. The OS allocates the IDT in memory, fills entry N with the address of the handler for interrupt N, and points the IDTR register at the table's base. From then on the Hardware interrupt path uses the IDT to dispatch every interrupt without any further software help.

Each entry — a gate descriptor on x86 — carries enough information for the CPU to switch into the handler safely: the handler's linear address, the code segment to load (which sets the new privilege level, typically ring 0), and a type byte distinguishing interrupt gates (which mask further interrupts on entry) from trap gates (which don't). Entries 0–31 are reserved for CPU-defined exceptions (divide-by-zero, Page fault, double fault, ...); 32–255 are available for device IRQs and software interrupts.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The IDT is one of three OS-installed tables that the CPU consults transparently — the others are the GDT (segment descriptors) and the page tables. Each is a hardware contract: the OS lays bytes out in a format the silicon understands, then hands over a pointer.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A small OS-allocated structure that the CPU reads to find the kernel stack pointer when it raises privilege. Its historical purpose — full task switching in hardware — is dead on modern x86-64; what survives is one essential field: RSP0, the address of the kernel stack to switch to on entry from ring 3.

During the Hardware interrupt path, the moment the CPU decides to push RIP/RSP/CS/RFLAGS, it reads RSP0 from the current TSS (located via the GDT entry pointed at by the TR register) and switches RSP to that value before pushing. If the TSS were missing or pointed at user memory, the very first interrupt would either fault while trying to push, or push the user's registers onto attacker-controlled memory. So the TSS is set up at boot, before interrupts are enabled.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. This is why a kernel needs a per-CPU TSS, not a per-process one: RSP0 is consulted on every transition into the kernel, and each CPU needs its own kernel stack to land on.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Machine-instance notes — concepts these chips introduced live in their own atomic notes; this tree records what was novel about each implementation.
Concepts in play:

html:ul.html:li: Cache hierarchy — Pentium adopted L1/L2 caching and split the L1 into separate instruction and data caches.
Branch prediction — branch history table (BHT) and branch target buffer (BTB) combined with static prediction as a fallback on BHT miss.
Speculative execution — execute past a predicted branch, write back only after the prediction is confirmed.

html:ol.html:li: Fetch / Prefetch: read from instruction cache.
Decode 1: decode to internal format; branch prediction.
Decode 2: convert complex instructions via Microarchitecture to Micro-operations.
Execute.
Write.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Back-end: two integer ALUs (also doing address calculations) and one floating-point ALU. The FP register file was arranged as a stack, with most FP ops operating on the top — zero-cycle stack swaps existed but still occupied a decode slot.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Estimated 30% of Pentium transistors went to legacy support, mostly the microcode ROM.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Concepts introduced:

html:ul.html:li: out-of-order execution — instructions execute in dataflow order, commit in program order.
Reorder buffer — 40-entry ROB tracks all in-flight instructions and assists register renaming.
Reservation station — three-instruction dispatch, up to five-issue per cycle.
CISC vs RISC decoupling — x86 in front, Micro-operations internally.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Simple and complex decoders are split because many CISC instructions translate to one or two micro-ops — the simple decoders handle those in parallel with the slow complex decoder.
Remark. 40% of Pentium Pro transistors went to backward-compatibility support.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Machine-instance notes for the PowerPC line — a RISC implementation of the same concepts covered in superscalar and Branch prediction, with a few innovations specific to this family.
Front-end. standard 4-stage pipeline. Instruction queue dispatches 3 instructions per cycle.

html:ul.html:li: Identifies unconditional branches (and resolved conditional ones) in the queue; if taken, deletes the branch instruction and starts fetching from the target — branch folding.
Untaken branches "fall out" — fall through.
No Reorder buffer-equivalent. Static scheduling.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Back-end. one integer ALU (also handles addresses), one floating-point ALU with single-cycle fused multiply-add. For FP-heavy code the integer ALU just does addresses.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Competed with two-integer-ALU designs because simpler logic left room for 4× the L1 cache (32KB vs 8KB).

html:ul.html:li: Branch dispatch on a separate bus, so branches don't compete with non-branch dispatch.
Up to 2 non-branch instructions dispatched per cycle.
Adds a commit unit and completion queue — the PowerPC names for Reorder buffer. Dynamic scheduling with an instruction window.
Two added execution units: a system unit for misc integer ops and a load/store unit. Lighter load on the integer ALU.
FPU took hits: 3 instructions every 4 cycles (mandatory bubble); double precision (including fmadd) takes 2 cycles in execute.
Splits decode/dispatch and writeback into two stages — enables shorter clock cycles.
Up to 6 instructions dispatched per cycle.
Dynamic Branch prediction with a 512-entry BHT (2 bits per entry) and a 64-entry branch address cache (BTB).
Adds a condition-register unit sharing dispatch bus with the branch unit.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Back-end. two simple integer units + one complex; fully pipelined FPU.
Dispatch obeys Reservation station rules: in-order dispatch, execution-unit availability, ROB availability, and rename-register availability.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Shallow pipeline → small ROB and few reservation stations are acceptable. Tradeoff: lower clock speed.
Remark. The 604e doubled L1 to 64KB and added a condition-register unit, freeing the integer and branch units from condition computations.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Same 4-stage pipeline as the 603e. Two integer units instead of one; 3-cycle single-precision FP; 6-entry instruction queue, up to 4 fetched per cycle.

The 750's signature trick: a branch target instruction cache (BTIC). A traditional BTB stores the address of the branch target — you still have to re-fetch that instruction. The BTIC caches the actual first instructions at the target, eliminating the re-fetch latency. This makes Branch prediction dramatically more useful.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Did not support a SIMD instruction set.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Added a vector permute unit (VPU) and vector ALU (VALU) for SIMD; full double-precision FPU; 8-entry instruction queue.
Two contrasting approaches to using a transistor budget, well-paired as instance studies because they target the same constraints from opposite directions.
Optimizes for instruction-level parallelism. Seven-stage pipeline.

html:ol.html:li: Additional second fetching stage.

Issue stage. To avoid in-order issue stalls, the G4e uses issue queues (general / vector / float) that issue 3, 2, and 1 instruction per cycle respectively. Issue queues differ from Reservation stations in that each queue feeds a group of execution units, not a single one.
Complete stage.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Optimizes for clock speed. 20-stage pipeline — extreme depth for fast clock, with the obvious downside that mispredicted branches are very expensive.

html:ul.html:li: Needs deep buffers (126-entry Reorder buffer) to keep the fast back-end fed.
x86 decoding was too slow to keep a 20-stage pipeline fed.
The trace cache moves to L1, storing already-decoded Micro-operations traces; the raw instruction cache moves to L2.
Trace cache is indexed during Branch prediction, so speculative paths past a branch can be stored in the same trace.
Operates in two modes — execution mode (trace hit, feed micro-ops directly) and build mode (trace miss, decode and assemble while feeding forward).

fr:title.text: Pentium M, Core Duo, Core 2 Duo

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Pentium M, Core Duo, Core 2 Duo

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The post-Pentium-4 generations, designed under the Power wall. Each generation contributed an innovation to the concept stack.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A processor often has three names: a brand name, a microarchitecture brand name, and a microarchitecture code name.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: First chip explicitly designed for low power. Innovations:

html:ul.html:li: Widened fetch buffer to 64 bytes — decodes up to 4 16-byte x86 instructions at a time.

Micro-op fusion — pairs of Micro-operations from the same x86 instruction (e.g. store-address + store-data, or load + ALU op for a read-modify) are tracked together as one queue entry until issue. See Micro-operations.
Loop detector in Branch prediction — counters watch loop trip counts; loops that iterate the same number of times each invocation can be predicted with 100% accuracy.
Indirect predictor — like a BHT but stores each branch's favorite target address.
Stack execution unit — dedicated unit with its own adder to handle push/pop ESP updates without going through the integer ALU. Stack micro-ops never reach the back-end.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The first true Multicore x86: two cores on the same die, sharing L2. Innovations relative to Pentium M:

html:ul.html:li: Every decoder is capable of micro-op fusion (up to 3 fused entries into the micro-op queue per cycle).
Fuses vector SSE instructions in addition to scalars.
Improved loop detector for short loops.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Until this point, the "core" was an abstraction — front-end + back-end + L1. The Core Duo is when "core" became a thing chips have more than one of.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Theme: "more is always better" — wider decode/dispatch/issue, bigger Reorder buffer, more Reservation stations, more decode logic. Two innovations made the extra resources pay off:

html:ul.html:li: Macro fusion (front-end) — fuse pairs of x86 instructions (e.g. compare + branch) before decode. Max decode bandwidth grows to 5 x86 instructions per cycle without burning ROB or RS entries.

Memory disambiguation (back-end) — apply Speculative execution to memory aliasing. Loads can speculatively execute ahead of stores with unknown target addresses; on actual alias detected at commit time, the load and dependent ops are squashed and re-executed.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The point of memory disambiguation: stores must commit in program order relative to other stores, and no load can commit ahead of an aliased store. But the rule "no load past a store-data with unknown target" forced a lot of unnecessary stalls — most unknowns aren't actually aliases. Speculate and verify.
Back-end also gained single-cycle vector ops: a 128-bit data bus to the vector ALU replaced the previous 80-bit bus, so 128-bit SSE instructions decode as a single micro-op.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A load is one micro-op because address computation and memory access are inherently serial. A store is two micro-ops (store-address + store-data) because address computation and reading the register file can happen in parallel.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: n-bit computing refers to how many bits a register can hold. 64-bit means registers hold 64 bits, 64-bit instructions means their operands are 64 bits wide.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Instruction encoding size hasn't actually changed — the PC widened because the virtual address space did.
Remark. Only integer and addressing units widened. Floating point and vector units already operated on wider registers.

html:ul.html:li: Increased dynamic range — values can range over 2^{64} instead of 2^{32}. Overflow/underflow happens at the extremes of the representable range.
64-bit address space. x86-64 currently implements 40-bit virtual addresses but the architecture allows far more.
Drawback: pointers now take up twice the cache space.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. It is not always beneficial to use 64-bit integers. If you don't need the range, the wider type wastes cache space. The default integer size on x86-64 is still 32 bits; widening requires a one-byte prefix, adding less than 10% to code size.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Part of my revisiting CS fundamentals journey
The process abstraction, the syscalls for working with processes on Unix, the hardware-software contract that makes time-sharing safe, and the scheduling policies that decide who runs when.
A running program, plus all the state the OS needs to pause it and resume it later. A process consists of:

html:ol.html:li: Memory: an Address space holding the program's code, stack, and heap.
Registers: general-purpose registers carrying current computation, plus special-purpose registers:

html:ul.html:li: Program counter (PC): the next instruction to run.
Stack pointer and frame pointer: where the process sits on its stack.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A process moves between three states:

html:ul.html:li: running: currently on a CPU.
ready: runnable, waiting for the Scheduling to give it a CPU.
blocked: waiting on I/O or another event, not runnable.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Threads (see Concurrency Intro) share an address space within a process but each have their own PC, register set, and stack.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The minimum operations any OS must expose for working with Processes:

html:ol.html:li: Create: load a program from disk, set up its address space (stack, heap, code), and jump to main. The OS also initializes argc/argv.
Destroy: kill a misbehaving or unwanted process.
Wait: block until another process completes — useful for ordering dependent work.
Miscellaneous control: e.g. suspend / resume.
Status: report on running processes.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The stack is allocated up front. The heap starts small and grows via malloc, which calls into the OS (brk/sbrk) when it runs out of headroom.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: On Unix, the concrete realization of this API uses fork(), wait(), and exec() — covered separately.

The kernel-side data structure holding all per-process state. There is one PCB per Process, threaded together into the process list. The PCB is what the OS reads and writes during a Context switch: the saved register set lives here, as does the process state, the open files, the parent pointer, and so on.
An example from xv6:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Threads need an analogous thread control block; see Concurrency Intro.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The Unix system call that duplicates the calling Process. After fork() returns, two processes exist where there was one. The child gets its own copy of:

html:ol.html:li: Address space.
General-purpose registers.
Program counter — the child returns from fork() at the same instruction the parent does.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The two processes are distinguished by fork()'s return value:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Which process is scheduled first after fork() is non-deterministic. Use wait() if you need ordering.
Remark. The copy is logical, not physical: real implementations use Copy-on-write so the duplicated address space only allocates fresh pages when one side writes to them.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Blocks the calling Process until one of its children terminates. The OS marks the child's PCB ZOMBIE on exit, keeping enough state (exit status, resource usage) for the parent to retrieve via wait(); only when the parent collects it is the child fully reaped.
wait() returns the PID of the child that died. Variant waitpid(pid, ...) blocks on a specific child — useful when a parent has multiple children and only one is interesting.
Used to enforce ordering between parent and child code paths after fork(), or to know when an exec()'d program finishes.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Calling wait() with no children to wait on returns -1.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Replaces the calling Process's program with a new executable. exec() does not return on success — the original code, data, stack, and heap are discarded and the new program runs in their place. The PID is unchanged.
What exec() does internally:

html:ol.html:li: Loads the new executable's code and static data, overwriting the current.
Re-initializes stack and heap.
Jumps to the new program's entry point with arguments passed as argv.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The canonical Unix pattern is fork() then exec() — the shell uses this to launch a command without becoming that command:
Redirection is implemented by opening a file in the child between fork() and exec(), attached to STDOUT_FILENO:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The many variants — execl, execle, execlp, execv, execvp, execvP — differ in how they take arguments (list vs. vector), whether they use the PATH environment variable, and whether they take an explicit environment.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An asynchronous notification delivered to a Process. A process sends a signal with kill(pid, signum) (the name is misleading — most signals don't kill); the recipient can install a handler with signal(signum, handler) to "catch" it, otherwise the default action runs (often termination).

Signals are the OS's mechanism for events the recipient didn't ask to be told about: SIGINT from Ctrl-C, SIGSEGV from a Page fault on an invalid page, SIGCHLD when a child exits, SIGTERM from an administrative kill.
Who can signal whom depends on the user owning the process — a user can signal their own processes; the superuser can signal any process.

The OS lets user code run directly on the CPU for speed, but with hardware-enforced limits that prevent a buggy or malicious program from compromising the rest of the system. Two questions motivate the mechanism:

html:li.html:em: How do we make sure a program doesn't do what we don't want it to do?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The first question is answered by hardware User mode vs kernel mode enforced via the Trap table, with a controlled Syscall mechanism as the only path from one to the other. The second is answered by periodic timer interrupts that trap to the OS, which performs a Context switch and runs the scheduler.
The whole stack is bootstrapped during the Boot sequence, when the kernel installs the trap table and starts the timer before handing control to user space.

Two privilege levels supported by the CPU. User mode restricts the running process to a limited set of instructions and a limited view of memory — usually just its own address space. Kernel mode runs the OS with full access to all instructions and all of physical memory.

Switching between modes is mediated by hardware via a Trap table populated at boot. The OS sets up the table; the CPU enforces it. A process cannot promote itself to kernel mode arbitrarily — it must invoke a Syscall mechanism, which traps to a fixed entry point in the OS.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Kernel mode is dangerous because the address space is effectively the entire machine. The mode bit + the trap table together are what makes it safe to time-share a CPU between mutually distrusting programs.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A hardware-readable table installed by the OS at boot that maps each kind of exceptional control-flow event (syscall, interrupt, page fault, illegal instruction, ...) to the address of an OS handler. When the CPU takes a trap, it consults this table to decide where to jump, switches into kernel mode, and runs the handler.

The table is what makes it safe to expose privileged operations to user mode: user code never gets to pick the destination of a mode-switch, only the trap number. The OS controls every entry point, the CPU enforces the indirection.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Installing the trap table is itself a privileged operation. It happens once, early in boot, before any user process exists.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A syscall is a user-mode-initiated request for a privileged service from the kernel. Because user code cannot jump into kernel code directly, the mechanism is built around a single trap instruction plus a pre-agreed register convention identifying which service to perform.
The end-to-end sequence:

html:ol.html:li: User code places the syscall number in a pre-defined register and invokes the trap instruction.
Hardware saves enough state to resume the process and switches to kernel mode.
Hardware jumps to the OS-installed handler via the Trap table.
The handler reads the syscall number from the agreed register and dispatches to the corresponding OS routine.
The OS validates the request, performs the work, and arranges return values per the calling convention.
The OS restores user state and returns the process to user mode.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Because the register convention is fixed by ABI, the user-mode side of a syscall is typically hand-written in assembly within libc.
Remark. Interrupts work via a similar trap-handler mechanism, but are initiated by hardware (timer, device) rather than user code.
Remark. The kernel must validate any pointers or arguments the user supplies — user code cannot be trusted not to ask the kernel to read kernel memory on its behalf.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The operation of saving the full execution state of one process (or thread) and restoring another's so the CPU can resume it as if it had never been interrupted. On a trap into the kernel, the hardware stashes the user-visible state (PC, condition codes, stack pointer) into a known location, and the OS saves the remaining general-purpose registers as part of the switching code.
Two flavors of state are involved:

html:ol.html:li: User registers: the current program's context (general-purpose registers, stack vars).
Kernel registers: meta-information used to resume the trap correctly (PC at trap, syscall number, etc.).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Context switches are not free — they incur the cost of cache and TLB pollution in addition to the direct cost of saving/restoring state. This is the cost amortized by larger scheduling quanta and a recurring concern for any Concurrency system.
The fixed series of steps a machine performs from power-on until a usable operating system is running. Each stage runs from a known location and hands off control to the next.

html:ol.html:li: Power signal resets the CPU to a known state.
The CPU fetches from a hard-wired address — the BIOS (or UEFI firmware). The BIOS runs basic hardware checks (POST).
The BIOS loads and jumps to the bootloader (e.g. GRUB) from a known location on disk.
The bootloader places the kernel image at the correct address and jumps to its entry point.
The kernel performs early setup: installs the Trap table (a special privileged instruction), starts the timer interrupt, sets up memory management, and finally enters user space.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The hardware is hard-wired only to find the BIOS. Everything past that is software the user can replace, which is why dual-booting and custom bootloaders work.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The scheduler decides which runnable Process to put on the CPU at each moment. There is no single right answer because the metrics conflict:

html:ol.html:li: Turnaround time: time from arrival to completion, T_{\text {completion}} - T_{\text {arrival}}. Minimized by running short jobs first.
Response time: time from arrival to first scheduling. Minimized by frequent rotation.
Fairness: every job makes progress; nobody starves.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: First-in, first-out. Jobs are run to completion in the order they arrive. Trivially fair under any reasonable notion of fairness, but suffers from the convoy effect: a single long-running job at the head delays every shorter job behind it, inflating average turnaround time.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. If the first arriving job is much longer than the rest, all subsequent jobs wait at least its full duration before getting any CPU.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Shortest job first. Run the job with the smallest total work first, run-to-completion. If all jobs arrive at the same time, SJF provably minimizes average turnaround time.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Real workloads don't all arrive at t = 0. A long job that arrives slightly before a short one still gets to monopolize the CPU until it finishes — SJF degenerates to FIFO scheduling under sequential arrivals.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Removing the run-to-completion constraint and allowing preemption gives STCF scheduling.

Shortest time-to-completion first. A preemptive refinement of SJF scheduling: whenever the scheduler is invoked, it picks the job with the least remaining work. A short job arriving mid-execution can preempt a long one.
STCF is provably optimal for minimizing average turnaround time over all preemptive schedulers.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Long jobs starve in response time. The lowest-priority work might not get a CPU slice for a very long time, which is unacceptable for interactive workloads.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Cycle through all runnable jobs, giving each a fixed time slice — the stride, time slice, or quantum length — before preempting and moving to the next. The best you can do at minimizing response time: every job sees the CPU within at most N quanta where N is the runnable-job count.
The quantum length is a tradeoff:

html:ul.html:li: Short quantum: better response time, more frequent context switches, worse cache locality.
Long quantum: amortizes context-switch cost, worse response time.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Worst-case response time for N jobs at quantum S is S(N-1).
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Round-robin minimizes response time but is poor for turnaround time relative to STCF scheduling. A scheduler that wants both has to combine ideas — see Multi-level feedback queue.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A practical scheduler that approximates STCF scheduling's turnaround properties and Round-robin scheduling's response properties without requiring foreknowledge of job lengths. Maintain multiple priority queues; within a queue, schedule round-robin. Never service a lower-priority queue while a higher-priority queue has a runnable job.
The priority of a job is not assigned by the user — it is learned from behavior:

html:ul.html:li: New jobs enter at the top priority.
Each priority level has an allotment — the total CPU time a job may consume before being demoted.
Once a job exhausts its allotment at a level, it drops to the next level.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Short jobs that finish quickly never leave the top queue (good turnaround). Interactive jobs that yield often stay at the top (good response time). CPU-bound jobs sink to the bottom and get scheduled when nothing better is runnable.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Gaming the allotment: a job can issue I/O just before its allotment expires to avoid demotion, since I/O time doesn't count.
Solution. Charge the job's allotment for total time at the priority level, including time spent blocked.
Problem. Starvation of low-priority queues if higher queues never empty.
Solution. Periodically reset all jobs to the top priority. Cheap and effective.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A proportional-share scheduler. Each runnable job holds some number of tickets, drawn from a fixed total. On each scheduling decision, the scheduler picks a random ticket and runs the job that owns it. Over time, a job receives CPU time proportional to its share of the tickets.
The implementation is trivial: one random number per decision, plus a sum table. No global state about historical CPU usage is needed.
Mechanisms layered on top:

html:ul.html:li: Ticket currency — give each user a fixed number of tickets to split among their processes however they like.
Ticket transfer — a client process can hand tickets to a server to boost its priority while servicing the request.
Ticket inflation — a process can grow its share temporarily, with bounds set by trust.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The unsolved problem is how to assign tickets in the first place. The mechanism is simple; the policy is not.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: A deterministic version of the same idea is Stride scheduling, which avoids the variance of random sampling at the cost of needing global state.

A deterministic proportional-share scheduler. Each job has a stride inversely proportional to its ticket count (the more tickets, the smaller the stride) and a running pass counter that accumulates its stride every time it's scheduled.
The decision is:

html:ol.html:li: Pick the job with the lowest pass.
Run it for one quantum.
Increment its pass by its stride.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A job with twice as many tickets has half the stride, so its pass grows half as fast, so it gets scheduled twice as often. Same long-run behavior as Lottery scheduling, but with zero variance.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. New jobs need an initial pass value. Setting it to 0 lets them dominate; setting it to the global minimum lets them merge in cleanly but requires tracking that minimum.
Remark. Each context switch is not free. Choosing a longer time slice amortizes that cost at the price of response time.
Remark. I/O-bound jobs are commonly handled by splitting a job into its non-I/O segments and scheduling them individually — turning one long-but-mostly-blocked job into many short, runnable ones.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The address-space abstraction, the translation schemes that implement it, the algorithms that manage free space within a process, and what happens when physical memory is not enough.

We are now moving on from scheduling into the wonderful world of all things memory. The starting point is the address space abstraction — each process is handed its own private virtual memory and the OS, with hardware help, manages the mapping from virtual to physical addresses.
The progression of translation schemes covered in TPE:

html:ol.html:li: Base and bounds — one base+bound pair per process. Simple but wasteful.
Segmentation — per-region base+bound. Removes internal fragmentation, introduces external fragmentation.
Paging — fixed-size translation units. Eliminates external fragmentation at the cost of a translation table.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The chunk of memory the OS gives a process to use at its own discretion. Each process has its own address space, so that one process cannot read or overwrite another's program state. An address space typically contains three regions:

html:ol.html:li: Code: the program text.
Stack: local variables, function arguments, return values.
Heap: dynamically allocated memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Every address a process sees is virtual — not the location in physical memory. The hardware translates virtual addresses to physical ones at access time. Approaches to that translation include Base and bounds, Segmentation, and Paging.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The API the user sees is tiny: a way to get memory and a way to release it, exposed as malloc and free. The common bugs are forgetting to initialize, forgetting to free, freeing too early (dangling pointer), freeing twice (double free), and freeing memory not from malloc (invalid free).
Remark. malloc and free are library calls, not syscalls. The heap is pre-allocated; malloc extends it by invoking brk/sbrk when it runs out.
Remark. When a process dies, the OS reclaims its memory, so a bounded leak is not catastrophic — just bad practice.
Remark. You can also acquire an anonymous heap-like region directly via mmap.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The simplest hardware mechanism for Address space translation. The CPU has two dedicated registers per process — a base pointing to the start of the process's region in physical memory and a bound marking the end. Translation is just phys = base + virt, with a check that the result stays below bound. The unit performing this translation in hardware is the memory management unit (MMU).
The hardware must expose privileged instructions for the OS to set base and bound on a context switch, and trap on out-of-bound accesses.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. To support a large address space, base-and-bounds suffers significant internal fragmentation — the unused gap between the stack and heap is allocated but wasted. With 16KB total memory and 4KB-but-mostly-empty processes, you can run 4 processes when you'd really have room for 8.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Segmentation is the natural next step — give each region (code, stack, heap) its own base and bound so the gaps don't have to be allocated.

An Address space-translation scheme that gives each logical region of a process (code, heap, stack, ...) its own base and bound. Instead of allocating a single contiguous chunk with empty space between regions, the OS allocates each segment separately and on demand. A virtual address is split into a segment number and an offset within that segment:

The hardware uses the segment number to index a segment table, retrieves that segment's base and bound, and adds the offset. Per-segment protection bits make read-only code and no-execute data straightforward.
Segmentation also enables memory sharing — two processes can map the same physical segment (e.g. shared code) into both their address spaces.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The stack grows downward, so the bounds check has to be direction-aware based on the segment number.

Remark. Segmentation does not solve fragmentation; it pushes it from internal (per-process) to external (per-system). Variable-sized segments leave variably-sized holes in physical memory. The fix that eventually won is Paging.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Instead of allocating memory in variable-sized chunks (which fragments badly), paging divides virtual and physical memory into fixed-size pages and maps virtual pages to physical pages independently. This is the address-translation scheme that won.
The mechanism rests on three concepts:

html:ol.html:li: The Page table — per-process map from virtual page number to physical page number.
The Translation lookaside buffer — small hardware cache that absorbs the cost of consulting the page table on every access.
The Multi-level page table — the page table is itself paged, so processes only pay for the parts of the address space they use.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When a process touches a virtual address whose page isn't in physical memory, the page table's present bit is unset; the hardware traps to the OS as a page fault, and the OS retrieves the page from swap. The eviction policies that decide what to swap out are covered by Page replacement.

The two-level decomposition did not arrive fully formed. The Segmented page tables design tried to give each named segment of the address space its own variable-sized page table; the fragmentation that produced is what motivated the move to fixed-size paged page tables.

The per-process data structure that maps each virtual page number (VPN) to a physical page number (PPN). A virtual address is split into a VPN and an offset within the page; the page table translates the VPN, the offset is appended unchanged.
Each page-table entry (PTE) carries more than just a PPN:

html:ol.html:li: Valid bit: whether this VPN has memory allocated. Gaps between stack and heap need not be allocated.
Protection bits: read/write/execute permissions.
Present bit: whether the page currently resides in physical memory (vs. swap).
Dirty bit: has the page been modified since being paged in.
Reference bit: has the page been accessed (used by eviction policy).

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. The table gets very large. For a 32-bit address space with 4KB pages and 4-byte PTEs, a linear page table costs 2^{32}/2^{12} \cdot 4 = 2^{22} bytes — 4MB per process.
Problem. Translation requires accessing the table in memory, which is slow on every memory reference. Mitigated by the Translation lookaside buffer.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The size problem is mitigated by Multi-level page table, which only allocates the parts of the table that are actually used.
A small hardware cache of recent virtual-to-physical translations. Without it, every memory reference would incur the cost of walking the Page table in memory.
An access proceeds as:

html:ol.html:li: Extract VPN from the virtual address.
Check whether the VPN is in the TLB.
TLB hit: read PPN from the entry, append offset, access memory.
TLB miss: walk the page table (in hardware on CISC, by trapping to OS on RISC), insert the translation into the TLB, retry.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A TLB entry holds the VPN, the PPN, a valid bit (separate from the PTE valid bit — invalid on init, eviction, context switch), and protection bits.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Each process has different translations for the same VPN, so a naive TLB must be flushed on every context switch.
Solution. Add an address space identifier (ASID) to each TLB entry and an ASID register set on context switch. Now entries from different processes coexist.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: "Ram is not always ram". Referring to the fact that a physical memory access takes a substantial different amount of time depending on whether it hit in the TLB.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. In real software-managed TLBs, an entry has global bits for shared pages, an 8-bit ASID, coherence bits, and a wired register reserving slots for code that cannot afford a TLB miss (e.g. the TLB-miss handler itself).
Remark. For very fast caches, even a TLB access is a bottleneck. Virtually indexed caches sidestep the TLB on the index path.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An early translation scheme that splits the virtual address space into a small number of named segments (code, data, stack, heap, ...) and gives each segment its own Page table sized only to the range of addresses that segment actually uses. The virtual address is interpreted as a triple: segment number, page number within the segment, and offset within the page.

Translation is a two-step walk. The segment number indexes a segment table, which yields the base and length of that segment's page table. The page number then indexes that page table to find the physical frame, and the offset is appended. Empty or unused segments need no page table at all, so a process pays for memory it actually uses — the same motivation that drives every non-flat translation scheme.

The mechanism has appealing side benefits. Each segment carries its own protection bits, so the architecture can enforce no-execute on the stack or read-only on the code segment at the segment-table level without per-page complexity. Translation overhead is bounded by the segment count, which is typically very small.

The problems are what killed it. Page tables are now variable-sized objects that still have to live in contiguous physical memory. The stack segment's page table might be small; the heap's might be large and grow over time. Allocating, growing, and freeing variably-sized tables in kernel memory produces external fragmentation — exactly the headache pure segmentation has at the user level, now pushed down a layer. If the heap outgrows its current page table, the kernel has to allocate a larger contiguous chunk and copy.

Secondary issues compound. Translation costs two memory references (segment table, then page table) before reaching data, which hurts without a Translation lookaside buffer. The segment count is fixed by the architecture, which is awkward for programs whose layout does not fit "code/data/stack/heap" — a sprawling memory-mapped region or a JIT wanting many small protection domains has nowhere to go. And segmentation tends to leak into the programming model: x86 carried segment registers around for decades after they had stopped being useful.

The fix that won is the Multi-level page table — page the page table itself, in fixed-size chunks, and recover the "only pay for what you use" property without variable-sized contiguous allocations. Every modern architecture descends from that decision.

A page table that is itself paged: instead of one linear array of PTEs, the table is split into page-sized chunks indexed by a top-level page directory. Subtrees of the address space that are entirely unused get a single null entry at the top level — no second-level page is allocated for them. This is the design that displaced Segmented page tables — it preserves the "don't pay for empty regions" property while keeping every piece of the structure page-sized, eliminating the variable-allocation fragmentation that doomed the segmented version.
Advantages over a linear Page table:

html:ol.html:li: Fits easily in memory — only allocate a second-level page when an entry in it is used.
Memory cost is proportional to the amount of address space actually in use.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. On a Translation lookaside buffer miss, the walk now costs two memory loads — page directory entry first, then the PTE.
Remark. Real architectures push this further (x86-64 uses 4 or 5 levels) and lean on the TLB to absorb the walk cost.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Once swap space is in play, the OS must decide which pages to evict from physical memory. The mechanism is the Page fault machinery; the question of policy is what this hub covers.

Physical memory is finite, so the OS reserves a region of disk — swap space — to hold pages that don't fit. Pages currently on disk have their present bit cleared in the Page table entry; the PTE instead holds a disk address.
When a process touches a virtual address whose page is not present, the hardware traps to the OS. This is a page fault. The handler:

html:ol.html:li: Looks at the PTE: if invalid, the access is illegal — terminate the process.
If valid but not present: read the page in from swap, update the PTE, retry the access.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The hardware never handles page faults itself — that would require it to understand disk I/O and the OS's swap layout. Page-fault handling is always in software.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The three cases on every memory access:

html:ol.html:li: Present + valid: hardware reads the PPN from the PTE and proceeds.
Not present + valid: hardware invokes the page-fault handler to retrieve from swap.
Not valid: hardware raises an exception; the OS typically kills the process.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. What happens when you dereference a nullptr? The TLB misses on VPN 0, the page-table walk finds the PTE marked invalid, and the hardware traps to the OS — which delivers SIGSEGV.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Least recently used is the standard heuristic for picking a page to evict: throw out the one that has been untouched the longest. It works because of the principle of locality — recently-used pages are likely to be used again soon (temporal locality).
Pure LRU requires updating a data structure on every memory access, which is too expensive. The clock algorithm approximates it cheaply:

html:ol.html:li: Maintain a reference bit per page, set by hardware on access.
Loop a pointer around the pages. If the reference bit is 1, clear it and advance.
If the reference bit is 0, evict that page.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. LRU is not optimal. The pathological case is the looping workload: if the working set is one page larger than the cache, LRU misses 100% of the time.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The quality of any replacement policy is measured by its average memory access time:
where T_P is physical-memory access time and T_D is disk access time. The disk gap is so large that even tiny miss rates dominate AMAT.
A desirable property of a page-replacement policy is the stack property: increasing the cache size never makes the miss rate worse. Surprisingly, not every reasonable-looking policy has it.

FIFO eviction violates the stack property — on certain access sequences, growing the cache strictly increases the number of misses. This is Belady's anomaly. It is what motivates LRU and the clock algorithm-style policies, which do have the stack property because they only depend on relative access recency.

The optimal eviction policy (offline) is to evict the page whose next use is farthest in the future. Unimplementable in practice (you don't know the future), but useful as a theoretical upper bound to compare against.

Two refinements make eviction practical on real workloads. A pure global policy lets one greedy process hog memory, which Segmented FIFO fixes with a per-process resident-set cap; and writing victims back one page at a time wastes disk bandwidth, which Page clustering amortizes by batching the dirty list.

A process-aware refinement of LRU and the clock algorithm. Pure FIFO is a global policy — it does not distinguish memory by owner, so one greedy process can evict everyone else's pages. Segmented FIFO instead maintains a per-process FIFO queue capped at that process's resident set size, bounding how much physical memory any single process can hold.
Evicted pages are not discarded outright; they move to one of two secondary lists:

html:ol.html:li: Clean page free list — evicted pages that were never modified.
Dirty page list — evicted pages that must be written back before reuse.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The secondary lists act as a victim cache: if a process faults on a page still sitting on the dirty list, it can be reclaimed without going to disk. The resident-set cap gives fairness; the secondary lists soften the cost of capping it too aggressively.

An optimization where two address spaces share the same physical page until one writes to it. Both processes have PTEs pointing at the same PPN, but with the write bit cleared. The first write traps to the OS, which allocates a fresh page, copies the contents, points the writing process's PTE at the new page (with writes re-enabled), and resumes.
Used pervasively:

html:ul.html:li: fork() — the child shares the parent's pages until either modifies one. Avoids the cost of copying an entire address space the child often immediately overwrites with exec().
Shared libraries — many processes map the same library text pages.
Kernel mapped into every process — same physical kernel code seen from every address space.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When a process asks for new heap memory, the OS must guarantee the returned pages contain zeros (to avoid leaking other processes' data). The naive implementation zeroes the page at allocation time. On-demand zeroing defers that work to first touch: until the process actually accesses a freshly-allocated page, no zero-fill happens.

The OS marks the page as not-present in the Page table. The first access traps as a Page fault, the handler zeroes a physical page, installs it in the PTE, and resumes. Heap memory that is allocated but never used costs nothing.

An amortization trick for the write side of page replacement. Writing one dirty page to swap at a time pays a full disk I/O per eviction, and small random writes are the worst case for a disk. Page clustering batches them: the OS collects a group of dirty pages from the global dirty list and writes them out together in a single larger, more sequential I/O. The per-page cost of paging out falls because the fixed overhead of a disk write is spread across the whole batch.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A common convention is to map the kernel into every process's address space (via Copy-on-write) so kernel code and structures appear at the same virtual addresses everywhere.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Once we have an Address space, we still have to actually allocate physical bytes within it. Free-space management is the discipline of tracking which bytes are free, picking which free chunk to satisfy a request, and merging adjacent freed chunks back together. Two fragmentation hazards to watch:

html:ol.html:li: External fragmentation: many small unallocated non-contiguous chunks.
Internal fragmentation: allocated chunks larger than what the requester uses.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Simplifying assumptions for the algorithms below: allocations are immovable once placed, total memory is fixed, and the user does not specify a size in free. The last assumption is handled by storing the size in a header preceding the returned pointer.
Given a free list of unallocated memory chunks and a request of size n, which chunk do you carve the allocation out of? Different answers trade off fragmentation against search time.

html:ol.html:li: Best fit: find the smallest chunk \geq n. Minimizes wasted space per allocation but tends to leave many small unusable fragments behind, and requires scanning the whole list.
Worst fit: find the largest chunk. Aims to leave usefully-sized leftovers but also requires a full scan.
First fit: take the first chunk that fits. Fast (early exit) but biases small fragments toward the start of the list.
Next fit: like first fit, but resume the search from where the previous allocation ended. Spreads small fragments more evenly.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: On free, the allocator searches the list for adjacent free chunks and coalesces them into a larger chunk. The size of each allocation is stored in a header immediately preceding the returned pointer so free doesn't need a size argument.

A data-structure-level optimization for allocators that maintains separate free lists per common object size. Instead of one global free list scanned by Malloc placement policies, you keep a list per size class. Allocations of that size pop straight off the matching list — no search.

html:ul.html:li: Grow a per-size list by requesting larger segments from the main allocator and chopping them up.
Drain back to the main list when no references remain.
Optionally leave objects pre-initialized on the list to skip constructor/destructor calls.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. This is the structure behind kernel slab allocators and many object-pool patterns. It works when the workload allocates many objects of a few common sizes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An allocator for memory of total size 2^n that maintains a binary-tree of free blocks at every power-of-two size. To service a request, walk down to the smallest block \geq requested size; if no block of that size exists, split a larger one (and its "buddy" becomes free). On free, check whether the freed block's buddy is also free, and if so, coalesce upward — recursively.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Memory of size 2^n organized as a buddy system.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The key trick is that buddies differ by exactly one bit in their address at each level. To check whether a block's buddy is free, flip that bit and consult a bitmap. To coalesce upward, mask off the bit and recurse.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Internal fragmentation is bounded by 2× the request size (you may have to round up to the next power of two), but external fragmentation is essentially eliminated within the buddy region.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Threads, the hazards that come with sharing state between them, and the primitives that make sharing safe.

Concurrency comes from sharing: multiple Threads within a single process share the address space, which is what makes them cheap to spawn and to communicate between, and is also what makes them dangerous. When threads interleave on a shared variable, you get a Race condition. The fragile region is the Critical section, protected by synchronization primitives like Locks and Barriers.

An independent unit of execution within a Process. Each thread has its own program counter, register set, and stack — but shares the surrounding address space, file descriptors, and other process-level resources with its siblings.

To support a context switch between threads, the OS keeps a thread control block (TCB) per thread — the per-thread analog of Process control block. Each thread also gets its own stack within the process's address space.
Why threads instead of processes:

html:ol.html:li: Intra-process parallelism: parallelizable work within one program, without paying the context switch cost of separate processes.
Avoiding blocking I/O: one thread blocks waiting on I/O while others keep running.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Sharing an address space is exactly what makes threads cheap to communicate between and exactly what makes them dangerous — see Race condition and Critical section.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When the correctness of a program depends on the order in which concurrent Threads schedule, you have a race condition. The classic example: two threads each execute
fr:mainmatter.html:pre: mov addr reg // load add 1 reg // increment mov reg addr // store
fr:mainmatter.html:p: intending to increment a shared variable twice. If the OS interleaves the threads at the wrong instruction boundary:

html:ol.html:li: Thread 1 stores its incremented value.
Thread 2 stores its incremented value.
Thread 1 increments (in its register).
Thread 2 increments (in its register).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The result is a single increment, not two — both threads loaded the same starting value and the second store clobbered the first.
The fix is to prevent interleaving inside the code region that touches shared state — the Critical section — using a synchronization primitive like Locks.

A region of code that accesses shared state in a way that is incorrect if more than one Thread executes it concurrently. Critical sections are exactly the regions that need protection by a synchronization primitive: a Locks excludes other threads while one is inside; a Barriers forces all threads to reach the same point before any may continue.
The art of writing correct concurrent code is to identify critical sections and protect them — without making them so large that you lose all parallelism.

A lock is a synchronization primitive that grants mutually exclusive access to a critical section. The implementations below trade off coherence traffic, fairness, and memory in different ways. The naive locks (Test-and-set lock and Test-and-test-and-set lock) are simple but generate O(n) coherence traffic per release; queue-based locks (Ticket lock, Array-based lock, MCS lock) scale better at the cost of extra state per waiter.

The simplest spin lock. The acquire loop spins on an atomic test_and_set: it writes LOCKED and reads the previous value in one step. If the previous value was UNLOCKED, the caller has the lock; otherwise it tries again.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Major coherence traffic. Every spinning core must acquire write permissions and invalidate everyone else on each test_and_set, so contention scales badly with the number of waiters.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A refinement of the Test-and-set lock that reduces coherence traffic during the spin. Threads first perform a plain load to check whether the lock looks free, and only attempt the atomic test_and_set when it does. Spinning is then read-only, so cores can share the cache line.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Spinning is a read-only operation now.

Problem. When the lock is released, the line must be invalidated on every waiter, and they all simultaneously read and try to write. The contention is shifted from steady state to release time but is still O(n) in the number of waiters.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A fair spin lock based on two counters. Each arriving thread takes a "ticket" by atomically incrementing next_ticket and then spins until now_serving equals its ticket. Release simply increments now_serving. Threads acquire the lock in FIFO order, which prevents starvation but does not by itself reduce contention.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
acquire(lock_ptr): my_ticket = fetch_and_increment(lock_ptr->next_ticket) while (lock_ptr->now_serving != my_ticket); // spin
release(lock_ptr): lock_ptr->now_serving = lock_ptr->now_serving + 1
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Still O(n) coherence traffic on release: incrementing now_serving invalidates the line on every waiter, and they all reload it to recheck their ticket.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each waiter is assigned its own slot in an array and spins only on that slot. Release just flips the next slot to "go-ahead", so the cache line touched by release is the one read by exactly one other thread. Coherence traffic per release is constant rather than O(n), at the cost of one slot per waiter.

html:ul.html:li: Give each waiter its own "slot" to wait on.
Only need to set the next slot to "go-ahead" on release.
Constant coherence traffic.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. O(n) memory for the lock array, and padding each slot to 64b (one cache line) to avoid false sharing makes that cost real.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A scalable, list-based queue lock. The lock variable is a pointer to the tail of a linked list of per-thread nodes. Each thread enqueues its own node and spins on a flag in its own cache line. The releaser wakes only its direct successor, so coherence traffic per handoff is constant and memory cost is proportional only to threads currently waiting, not to the lock's fanout.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
acquire(lock): I->next = null predecessor = fetch_and_store(lock, I) if predecessor != nil: // some node holds the lock I->must_wait = true predecessor->next = I // predecessor must wake us repeat while I->must_wait // spin till lock is free
release(lock): if (I->next == null): // no known successor if compare_and_swap(lock, I, nil): // make sure... return // CAS succeeded; lock freed repeat while I->next == nil // spin to learn successor I->next->must_wait = false // wake successor

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A barrier holds threads at a synchronization point until all P participants have arrived, then releases them simultaneously. The naive implementation uses a shared counter that each arriving thread decrements; the last arrival resets the counter and the rest spin until it returns to P.

fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
global (shared) count : integer := P
procedure central_barrier if fetch_and_decrement(&count) == 1 count := P else repeat until count == P
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. When count == P, a fast thread can reach the next barrier and decrement count again before the slow threads observe the reset. The slow threads then spin forever waiting for a value that has already passed.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The fix is to give each thread a private local_sense that flips on each barrier. The global sense flag is flipped by the last arrival, and waiters spin on sense == local_sense, so a fresh barrier cannot be confused with a stale one.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
global (shared) count : integer := P global (shared) sense : bool := true global (private) local_sense : bool := false
procedure central_barrier local_sense = !local_sense if fetch_and_decrement(&count) == 1 count := P sense = local_sense else repeat until sense == local_sense

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Here I will document my progress for the week along with other miscellaneous things I feel like disclosing publicly.
